- The Single Income Trap: Why Your Job Is a Financial Risk in 2026: Kind: captions Language: en You know that quiet Sunday evening feeling? The weekend is fading into memory. The shadow of Monday morning starts to creep in. It is a subtle anxiety that settles in your stomach. You think about the week ahead. You picture your desk in your computer screen. You mentally walk through the meetings you have to attend. You feel a familiar sense of resignation. This is just how it is. This is what being an adult feels like. But what if that feeling is not just about the work itself? What if it is a deeper financial anxiety you cannot quite name? It is the unspoken fear that your entire world rests on a single pillar. One job, one boss, one paycheck. We are taught to see this as stability. We are told that a good job is the foundation of a secure life. But in the world of 2026, that is one of the most dangerous financial myths you can believe. My name is Jack and I obsess over the hidden scripts that run our financial lives. I analyze the systems that keep people trapped and explore the mental shifts required to break free. If you are ready to look beyond the conventional wisdom and build genuine financial resilience, then you are in the right place. Make sure you subscribe and click the thumbs up button if this video helps you see your income differently. Now, let's talk about the illusion of the single income stream. Picture a sturdy wooden table. It has four strong legs, one at each corner. You can place a heavy load on this table. It will not wobble. It will not collapse. It is stable and reliable. Now, imagine a different table. This one has only one leg right in the center. It might look stylish and modern. It can still hold a plate or a book if you are very careful. But what happens if you lean on it too hard? What happens if the floor is slightly uneven? What happens if that single leg develops a crack? The entire structure comes crashing down. Your one job is that single leg. For decades, this model seemed to work. Our grandparents could join a company, work hard for 40 years, and retire with a pension. The company took care of them. The single leg was made of solid oak, but that world is gone. It is a black and white photograph in a history book. In 2026, that single leg is made of balsa wood. Companies no longer offer loyalty. Pensions are a relic of the past, replaced by 401k plans that place all the risk on you. Automation is not a distant threat from a science fiction movie. It is happening right now, quietly replacing roles that were once considered safe. The global economy is more connected and volatile than ever before. A crisis on the other side of the world can lead to budget cuts in your local office. Your job is not a fortress. It is a temporary agreement. It is an arrangement that can be terminated with a single email. Think about the language we use. We talk about a job market. A market is a place of buying and selling. Your employer is buying your time and your skills. But in any market, demand can change without warning. Relying on one buyer for 100% of your income is not a strategy. It is a gamble. You are betting your entire financial life on the hope that your single employer will need you, value you, and be able to pay you forever. That is a terrifying bet to make and your brain knows this. Even if you do not consciously think about it. This is the source of that Sunday evening anxiety. It is the background radiation of financial fragility. When you have only one source of income, you operate from a position of weakness. Every decision is filtered through a lens of fear. You hesitate to ask for a raise you know you deserve. What if they say no? What if they see you as demanding and put you on a future layoff list? You tolerate a toxic work environment or a terrible boss. You cannot afford to leave. The risk of having no paycheck is too great. You say yes to extra projects you do not have time for. You work late nights and weekends sacrificing your health and relationships. You feel you have to prove your worth constantly just to protect that single income stream. This is not freedom. This is a golden cage. The paycheck feels like a reward, but it is also the chain that keeps you in place. Your potential for growth becomes capped by your employer's budget. Your creativity is limited to the tasks assigned to you. You cannot take a risk on a new idea. You cannot take a month off to travel and recharge. You cannot tell your boss what you really think. Your choices are constrained by your dependence on that single paycheck. Now, let's look at the alternative. Imagine that same Sunday evening, but this time something is different. You still have your main job, but you also have a small side business that brings in $500 a month. You own a few dividend stocks that pay you $50 every quarter. You have an affiliate marketing website that generates $100 a month in passive income. None of these streams are massive. They do not replace your main salary, but they change everything psychologically. The anxiety in your stomach is gone. It is replaced by a quiet confidence. Your main job is no longer your entire world. It is simply your largest income stream among several. That single table leg now has three other smaller supports around it. If the main leg gets a little wobbly, the table does not crash. It just tilts slightly. You have time to fix the main leg or strengthen the others. This is the power of diversification. We understand this concept with investments. No financial adviser would tell you to put all your money into a single stock. That would be incredibly foolish. Yet, most people put 100% of their earning potential into a single job. We are diversifying our assets, but not our ability to earn those assets in the first place. It is a fundamental flaw in modern financial planning. The shift that happens in your brain is profound. When you go into your performance review, you are no longer asking for a raise from a position of desperation. You are negotiating from a position of strength. You know that even if the worst happens and you lose this job, you are not starting from zero. You have other income flowing in. This confidence changes how you carry yourself. It changes how you speak. Your boss can sense that you are not desperate. This ironically makes you more valuable in their eyes. When that toxic co-worker makes a comment, you are more likely to address it calmly and directly. You are not afraid of rocking the boat because you have your own life rafts. When a new exciting opportunity comes along that might pay less initially but has huge growth potential, you can actually consider it. You are not trapped by the need to maintain your current salary at all costs. You have options. The word options is the true definition of wealth. It is not about owning a fancy car or a big house. It is about having the freedom to make choices that align with your values, not your fears. Multiple income streams are the engine of financial options. Now I can hear the objections already. But Jack, I do not have time to start something else. I work a demanding job and have a family. I am already exhausted by the end of the day. This is the most common and the most understandable objection. And it is based on a misconception. The goal is not to get a second full-time job. The goal is to build systems that generate income without requiring a constant input of your time. Think about the time you spend on low-value activities. How many hours a week do you spend scrolling through social media? How much time is spent watching television shows you will forget by next week? We are not talking about eliminating all leisure time. But what if you redirected just three or four hours per week? That is the length of two movies. Over a month, that is 12 to 16 hours of focused effort. Over a year, that is a significant amount of time to build something new. The issue is often not a lack of time, but a lack of focused energy and a clear plan. Another common objection is, I do not have any special skills. What could I possibly offer that someone would pay for? This is almost always a failure of imagination, not a lack of ability. You have decades of experience and knowledge that you take for granted. Are you the person friends always ask for advice about relationships? That is a potential coaching business. Are you unusually good at organizing closets and decluttering homes? That is a service people will gladly pay for. Do you know how to bake amazing sourdough bread? You could sell it at a local market or create an online course teaching others. Can you speak another language fluently? Tutoring services are in high demand. Are you an expert in a specific video game? You could stream on Twitch or create strategy guides. The skills do not have to be formal or certified. They just have to provide value to someone else. Make a list of everything you know how to do from fixing a leaky faucet to planning a child's birthday party. You will be shocked at the length of that list. Somewhere on that list is a potential income stream waiting to be born. The third big objection is fear. What if I fail? What if I invest time and money and get nothing in return? This fear is valid, but it is misdirected. You are focusing on the small risk of a side project failing, but you are ignoring the massive catastrophic risk of your single income stream disappearing overnight. Which failure is worse? Losing a few hundred and 20 hours on a project that did not work out, or losing your entire livelihood with no backup plan, building multiple income streams is a form of insurance. You pay a small premium in the form of your time and effort to protect yourself from financial ruin. Furthermore, the concept of failure needs to be reframed. If you launch a project and it does not make money, you have not failed. You have paid for an education. You have learned what does not work. You have gained new skills. You have made connections. Your second attempt will be smarter and more efficient. The only true failure is allowing the fear to paralyze you into doing nothing. So, how do you actually start? It feels overwhelming, like trying to climb a mountain. The key is to start with a single small step. The goal is not to make $1,000 next month. The goal is to make $1, just one. Making your first dollar outside of your primary job is a monumental psychological victory. It proves the concept. It shows you that it is possible. It rewires your brain to see opportunities instead of obstacles. Maybe you sell a used book online. That is a new income stream. Maybe you answer a paid survey. That is a new income stream. Maybe you pets sit for a neighbor for $20. That is a new income stream. Start with the smallest, easiest thing you can think of. This is what I call the income snowball. You start with a tiny ball of snow, that first dollar. Then you roll it forward. You take the skills or confidence from that first dollar and aim for $10. Then you aim for $100 a month. Let's break down the types of income streams you can build. They generally fall into three broad categories. The first category is active income from a second source. This is where you are still trading your time for money. Examples include freelancing, consulting, or taking on part-time work in the gig economy, driving for Uber, delivering food, or finding freelance writing gigs on platforms like Upwork. The advantage of this is that it is often the fastest way to start earning. The disadvantage is that it is not scalable. If you stop working, the money stops coming in. It diversifies your income source, but it is still reliant on your active time. This is a good first step, but it should not be the final destination. The second category is portfolio or investment income. This is where your money starts working for you. This includes dividend income from stocks, interest from bonds or high yield savings accounts, and capital gains from selling investments. This is a cornerstone of long-term wealth building. The beauty of this is that it is completely passive. Your money is doing the heavy lifting 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. The challenge is that it requires capital to start. You need money to make money. This is why it should be developed in parallel with other streams. The money you earn from your active side hustle can be funneled into your investment portfolio. This creates a powerful synergy. Your active work buys the assets that will one day pay for your freedom. The third category is the holy grail. Passive income from business or royalties. I want to be very clear here. Passive income is a misleading term. It does not mean you do nothing and money magically appears. It means you do the work upfront and the system you build continues to pay you over and over again with minimal ongoing effort. Think of a musician who writes a hit song. They do the work of writing and recording it once, but they earn royalties every time it is played on the radio or streamed online for years to come. Think of an author who writes a book. The intense work is done once, but the book can sell copies for decades. In the digital age of 2026, the opportunities for this are endless. You could create an online course teaching a skill you have mastered. You build the course once and it can be sold thousands of times. You could start a YouTube channel focused on a specific niche. The videos you create today can earn ad revenue for years. You could build a niche website and earn money through affiliate marketing. You write the articles once and they attract traffic and generate commissions over time. You could design and sell digital products like ebooks, templates, or stock photos. The cost of creation is your time, but the cost of replication is zero. This is the most scalable form of income. It decouples your earnings from the hours you work. This is where true financial leverage is created. An ideal strategy involves a blend of all three. You use your primary job, your main active income, to cover your living expenses and provide initial investment capital. You start a small active side hustle, category 1, to generate extra cash flow quickly. You then take that extra cash flow and systematically invest it into your portfolio, category two, and into building a passive income business, category three. This is not a get-rich quick scheme. This is a get-rich for sure system. It requires patience, discipline, and a long-term perspective. Most people will not do this. They will continue to complain about their job. They will continue to live with that quiet Sunday evening anxiety. They will hope for a raise or a promotion to solve their problems. But hope is not a financial plan. Let's talk about the mathematical reality of this. Imagine you earn $60,000 a year from your main job. After taxes, let's say your take-home pay is $48,000 or $4,000 per month. This entire $4,000 is dependent on one source. Now, let's say you build a small side business that nets you an extra $500 per month. You have not just increased your income by 12.5%. You have fundamentally changed your financial risk profile. Now, only 87.5% of your income is at the mercy of your employer. You have created a buffer. You have bought yourself a piece of freedom. What can you do with that extra $500? You could use it to pay off debt faster, freeing up even more cash flow. You could invest all of it into a lowcost index fund. At an 8% average annual return, that $500 per month becomes over $78,000 in 10 years. It becomes over $250,000 in 20 years. It becomes nearly $700,000 in 30 years. That small seemingly insignificant side income stream has the power to build generational wealth if you are patient. It transforms your financial trajectory from a straight line into an upward curving exponential graph. But the most powerful effect is not even mathematical. It is the shift in your identity. When you earn money from multiple sources that you created and control, you stop seeing yourself as just an employee. You are no longer just a marketing manager or a software developer. You become an entrepreneur. You become an investor. You become the chief executive officer of you incorporated. Your life is your business and you are in charge of its revenue streams. This mindset shift permeates every area of your life. You start seeing problems not as annoyances but as potential business opportunities. Your neighbor complains about how hard it is to find a reliable house cleaner. Your entrepreneur brain lights up. You read an article about the rise of a new social media platform. Your investor brain starts thinking about which companies will benefit. you become more resilient, more creative, and more in control of your destiny. This is the real reason you need multiple income streams in 2026. It is not just about the money. It is about reclaiming your power. It is about insulating yourself from the whims of a volatile job market. It is about transforming fear into confidence. It is about building a life where you have choices. The world has changed. The social contract between employers and employees is broken. Continuing to rely on a single source of income is like navigating a stormy sea in a raft with a slow leak. You might be fine for a while, but eventually you are going to sink. Building multiple income streams is like building a proper boat. It takes work. You have to gather the materials. You have to learn how to put them together. But once it is built, you are no longer at the mercy of the waves. You can choose your destination. You can navigate the storms. You are the captain of your own ship. Do not wait for a layoff to be your wakeup call. Do not wait for a medical emergency to reveal your financial fragility. The time to start building your second income stream is now. Start today. Start small. Sell one thing, offer one service, make $1. That $1 is the most important money you will ever earn because it represents a new beginning. It is the first brick in the foundation of your financial fortress. It is the proof that you are more than your job title. You are the architect of your own financial future. And that future begins not with a lottery win or a stock market miracle. It begins with a simple, powerful decision to create your second source of income. Trust me, your future self living with confidence and options will thank you for

You absolutely cannot protect your personal assets or keep the IRS off your back without a properly structured business entity. Most YouTubers and content creators run their channels as a sole proprietor, mixing their AdSense revenue directly with their personal bank accounts, and that's why they get crushed at tax time or leave themselves totally exposed to copyright claims. So, right out of the gate, here's the deal. If you're making any money at all from your YouTube channel, the government automatically views you as something called a sole proprietor. This is how basically everyone starts. But as you start to grow, it quickly becomes the most dangerous position you can be in. And this is where it gets really, really clear. Look at the difference. As a sole proprietor, there is zero legal separation between you and your channel. You are the business. So, if your channel gets sued, you get sued personally. your savings account, your car, your home, it is all fair game. An LLC, on the other hand, creates a totally separate legal person. Now, the business gets sued, not you. It's that simple. And it's the fundamental switch from being a hobbyist to a protected professional. All right, so let's talk about the solution. Let's look at how we build a firewall between your personal life and your business life. So, think of an LLC as like literal legal armor for your personal life. It tells the world and more importantly the law that your channel is its own thing separate from you. This means if a lawsuit pops up, let's say for a copyright or defamation, they sue the LLC. Only the assets owned by the business itself, like the money in its bank account, are at risk, not your personal stuff. So, you're ready to build your shield. Great. But here's the thing, and this is super important. Just having an LLC isn't enough. You have to create it and just as importantly, maintain it the right way. Getting this right is absolutely non-negotiable. Okay, let's walk through the blueprint step by step. First, pick a unique name that ends with LLC. Easy enough. Second, and this is key for your privacy, you appoint a registered agent. We'll talk more about that in a sec. Third, you file the official paperwork with your state. This will cost you a bit, maybe 50 bucks, maybe a few hundred depending on where you live. Once that's approved, you get a free EIN from the IRS. Think of it like a social security number, but for your business. Step five is creating an operating agreement. That's just your company's internal rule book. And finally, and I cannot stress this enough, you open a separate business bank account. This last step is the one that keeps your shield strong. Let's zero in on this registered agent thing for a sec because honestly, this is huge for creators. You could list your own home address, but that means your private address is now in the public record for anyone to find. Not great. For about a h 100 bucks a year, you can hire a professional service that lets you use their address instead. This is a gamecher for protecting your privacy and your personal safety. Okay, so where you set up your LLC actually matters a lot. Doing it in your home state is usually the simplest, but some states, like Wyoming, for example, have incredibly strong laws that give you even more privacy and make it way harder for someone to sue your LLC successfully. Since a YouTube channel is a digital business, you often have the flexibility to shop around and form your company in the state that gives you the best possible protection. Okay, the shield is built. Now for the fun part, the payoffs. And this isn't just about playing defense. It's about putting more money in your pocket and leveling up your entire operation. Right? Once you have that LLC, you can unlock this amazing tax strategy. It's called electing to be taxed as an S corporation. Now, this doesn't change your LLC legally. It's just a different way of telling the IRS how to handle your taxes. And for creators who are making a steady income, say over $40 or $50,000 a year in profit, this is where you can save some serious cash. And here's how it works. It's actually pretty brilliant. See, without it, every single dollar of profit you make gets hit with that nasty 15.3% self-employment tax. But with an escorp election, you split your income. You pay yourself a reasonable salary, what someone else would get paid to do your job, and you definitely want a CPA to help you figure out that number. Only that salary gets hit with the full tax. The rest of the profit, you can take that as a distribution, which is not subject to self-employment tax. For a growing channel, we're talking thousands, maybe even tens of thousands of dollars back in your pocket every year. And look, don't just take it from me. But it's not just about the legal stuff and the taxes. An LLC completely changes the game professionally. It signals to brands and sponsors that you are serious. It builds instant credibility. It turns your channel from just a creative outlet into a real tangible asset that you could one day sell. I mean, every top creator you can think of, from Mr. Beast to Retin Link, run their entire empire through LLC's. Okay, so you've built the shield. You're feeling good, but listen up because this is where everything can go wrong. Setting up the LLC is only step one. If you don't maintain it correctly, if you start treating it like it's not a real separate thing, then a court can decide it isn't real either. And that legal shield you built, it will shatter. Yeah, there's a fancy legal term for this, piercing the corporate veil. That veil is the legal wall between you and your business. It's what keeps you safe. But if a judge decides you haven't been respecting that wall, they can just tear it down. And just like that, you are completely exposed again. It's like the LLC never even existed. Suddenly, your house, your car, everything is back on the table. So, how does this happen? Well, the number one way, the absolute biggest mistake creators make is mixing money. It's called co-mingling funds. Using your business bank account to pay your personal rent, that's a fatal error. Buying groceries with a company debit card, huge mistake. You have to maintain a clean, strict separation between your money and the business's money. No exceptions. That along with bad recordkeeping is the fastest way to destroy your own protection. So really, all of this boils down to one simple but massive mindset shift. And it's probably the most important thing to take away from this entire explainer. From this day forward, you are not entering into a brand deal. Your business is. The LLC signs the contract. the LLC is the one that gets paid. You are just an owner or an employee of that business. This separation isn't just some legal paperwork. It's a discipline you have to practice every single day. So, I'll leave you with this question, and your answer really changes everything. - Onlyfans : How One Greedy Decision Destroyed A $17B Lust Empire: Kind: captions Language: en At its peak, Only Fans was being valued at $17 billion in private discussions. $17 billion built on a 10,000 loan from a man's father. By 2025, that number had collapsed to $3.5 billion. An 80% drop. Not because of competition, not because of regulators, not because of the banks, because of one man, one decision, one catastrophic act of greed at the exact moment the platform had everything to lose. So, how does a business generating $7.2 2 billion in revenue end up worth a fraction of what it should be. Let's walk through the wreckage. Only fans didn't start as a pawn site. That's the part the narrative always skips. It started in 2016. Tim Stokeley, a British entrepreneur from Harlo, Essex with a 10,000 loan from his father guy who reportedly told him, "Tim, this is going to be the last one." The original pitch was clean, a subscription platform where creators of any kind, fitness coaches, musicians, chefs, could monetize directly from fans. No algorithm, no middleman, direct access. The model was elegant. Creators set their own prices. Only fans took a 20% cut. Everyone else got 80%. Simple, scalable, and for a while, boring. Then came the turning point no one planned for. Adult creators found the platform. Unlike traditional adult entertainment, where studios owned everything and performers got pennies, only fans gave them control. Full creative ownership, direct payment, no gatekeeper. The platform didn't go looking for the adult content industry. The adult content industry came looking for it. And in 2018, someone noticed. Enter Leonid Redvinsky, a Ukrainian American entrepreneur from Miami, the owner of My Free Cams, one of the internet's oldest adult streaming platforms. A man who understood better than anyone on Earth what it meant when consumers paid directly for Desire. Redvinsky acquired 75% of FEX International, the parent company of Only Fans, for an undisclosed sum. From the outside, the transaction barely registered. Inside, everything changed. Under Rinsky, the platform didn't just grow, it detonated. He understood a principle that Silicon Valley had spent decades ignoring. If you let creators earn more than anywhere else on the internet, they will bring their audiences with them. He didn't need a viral marketing campaign. He just needed a better revenue split than every competitor and he had one. Then COVID hit. March 2020. The world locked down and strip clubs closed. Film sets shuttered. Live events evaporated and millions of people and performers, entertainers, and people who had simply lost their jobs needed money. Only Fans was there. The growth wasn't gradual. It was vertical. Creators flooded in. Subscribers flooded in behind them. By 2021, the platform had 130 million registered users and over 2 million creators. Revenue was accelerating so fast that internal projections were being revised upward every quarter. This was the golden moment. The kind of inflection point that happens once in a company's history. The kind investors write case studies about music. And Radvinsky was extracting every dollar of it. In 2022 alone, he pulled $338 million in dividends out of the company. By 2023, that number hit $472 million. By 2024, he took $71 million in a single year. saw a total of over $1.8 billion pulled out of the business since 2021. Read that again. $1.8 billion taken out while everyone else was told the company was pursuing growth. But here's where the story turns dark. The banks were getting nervous, and you can understand why. In December 2020, a New York Times investigation exposed Pornhub for hosting videos of rape and child sexual abuse. The report was meticulous, damning, and impossible to ignore. Within days, Visa and Mastercard didn't issue a warning. They didn't request a remediation plan. They cut the site off entirely. No negotiation, no grace period, just gone. The adult content industry didn't just feel the tremors. It went cold overnight. Payment processors across the financial system began quietly reviewing every platform operating in the space on looking for the next liability they hadn't noticed yet. Only Fans was not immune to the scrutiny. It couldn't be. A BBC investigation published in May 2021 found that the platform was systematically failing to prevent underage users from appearing in explicit content, not edge cases, not isolated incidents. Systemic failure. A US watchdog subsequently confirmed that images of missing children had appeared on the platform. These weren't allegations from advocacy groups that could be dismissed or litigated. These were documented findings from credible institutions with the infrastructure to act on them. The reputational heat wasn't just uncomfortable anymore. It was becoming unbearable. And more importantly, it was becoming actionable. The banks moved. JP Morgan Chase, BNY Melon, Metro Bank. Accounts were being closed. The payments were being blocked. Transactions tied to adult content were being refused processing. The financial infrastructure that kept the entire machine running. The plumbing that moved billions of dollars from subscribers to creators to the company was threatening to collapse. Not theoretically, actively, in real time. Red Vinsky had a choice in front of him. A real one with real consequences either way. He could invest in serious content moderation. Build the compliance infrastructure that would satisfy regulators and reassure financial partners. The kind of rigorous, expensive, time-consuming work that actually fixes the problem. He could treat this as the wake-up call it was and build a platform that could survive institutional scrutiny. or he could find a shortcut. Something fast enough to calm the banks, invisible enough to satisfy the optics, but cheap enough not to interfere with the dividend flow. He chose the shortcut. August 19th, 2021 was the day the floor nearly fell out. Only Fans announced with just 6 weeks notice that it would ban all sexually explicit content effective October 1st. Not regulate it, not restrict it, not create a tiered system with enhanced verification requirements, ban it completely, permanently. The platform that had been built brick by brick on adult content. The platform where creators had been explicitly recruited with the promise of creative and financial freedom. The platform where over 90% of total revenue, billions of dollars, was generated by adult content, was going to ban adult content, music. The stated reason was compliance with banking partners. And that part was true. Now, as far as it went, the banking pressure was real, the regulatory exposure was real, and doing nothing was genuinely not an option. But buried beneath the compliance argument was something else entirely, something Redinsky wasn't saying publicly. He was simultaneously in the middle of trying to raise institutional investment at a valuation north of $1 billion. Mainstream venture capital firms, the kind who could fund a serious IPO pathway, wouldn't go near a platform synonymous with pornography. Not because of personal objections. Money doesn't have personal objections, but because the downstream compliance risk, the regulatory exposure, and the reputational association made it structurally impossible to justify to their own limited partners. The ban wasn't only about the banks. It was about optics. And it was about making the company legible and palatable to the kind of institutional capital that could turn Only Fans into something Red Rinsky could sell at a premium. It was about engineering the exit. The creators were a detail in that plan, an inconvenient one. The creators did not see themselves as a detail. They revolted. Within 48 hours, the internet didn't just push back. It erupted. Sex workers who had spent years building audiences, producing content, and structuring their entire financial lives around Only Fans income were now being told the platform was pivoting away from them with 6 weeks notice. Unsle parents who had used the platform to survive the economic devastation of the pandemic. People for whom Only Fans wasn't a side hustle, but a lifeline were told their income stream was being shut down on a corporate timeline that had nothing to do with them. Professional performers who had left traditional adult studios specifically because Only Fans gave them direct control over their work, their image, and their earnings. People who had made a values-based decision to work independently were watching that decision get invalidated overnight. The organizing happened fast. Creators coordinated across Twitter, Reddit, and private messaging groups. Mainstream journalists who had spent years covering creator economy issues picked up the story immediately. Civil liberties organizations weighed in. Known the backlash wasn't just loud. It was structured, documented, and sustained. It didn't burn hot for a day and fade. It kept going. 6 days later, on August 25th, 2021, Only Fans reversed the decision entirely. The company tweeted that they had secured assurances necessary to support our diverse creator community. The ban was suspended indefinitely. adult content would remain on the platform. Six days. That's how long the entire plan lasted. Six days from announcement to full reversal. The compliance argument that had been framed as a structural business necessity, the kind of decision companies describe as painful but unavoidable, dissolved in less than a week under the weight of creator backlash and public pressure, which raised an obvious question that nobody in the press asked loudly enough at the time. If the banking pressure was so severe that it required banning the core product with six weeks notice, how exactly did it get resolved in six days with a tweet? What assurances were secured? From whom? On what terms? What changed between August 19th and August 25th that made the existential banking threat suddenly manageable? The answer, most likely, is that the threat was always more manageable than the announcement implied. The ban was a negotiating position dressed up as a compliance necessity, a way to signal to institutional investors that management was willing to make hard choices, even if when tested, it turned out they weren't. What Radvinsky didn't fully calculate was the cost of the signal itself. Now, but here's what the reversal actually cost. The trust was gone. Not damaged, not strained, gone. Creators who had built their entire livelihoods on the platform, who had turned down corporate jobs, relocated their lives, structured their finances around Only Fans income, now knew with absolute certainty that management would throw them under a bus the moment it was financially convenient. That knowledge never left. You can't unring that bell. Once a platform proves it will abandon you when the pressure gets high enough, you never look at it the same way again. The relationship changes permanently. And in the creator economy, that relationship is everything. Competing platforms didn't waste a single day. Fansley, Patreon, emerging alternatives. They immediately launched aggressive creator recruitment campaigns. Now, we will never ban your content. It was the easiest marketing message in history. They didn't need a strategy deck. They didn't need a branding agency. They just needed to point at what Only Fans had just done and say, "We're not them." Thousands of creators quietly began building parallel presences on other platforms. not leaving entirely. The audience was still there, but hedging, diversifying, preparing for the next betrayal they now assumed was coming. The brand, which had been synonymous with creator empowerment, was now synonymous with creator betrayal. That's not a PR problem you fix with a press release. That's a structural identity shift. The story of Only Fans in every future article, every investor pitch, every regulatory hearing now had a chapter called the week they tried to abandon their creators. That chapter doesn't disappear. Meanwhile, Radvinsky had burned his chance at institutional investment without actually getting the institutional investment. Think about that for a moment. He paid the full price of the reversal. The trust damage, the brand damage, the creator exodus risk, the competitor momentum without capturing any of the upside he was chasing. The IPO pathway was closed. The mainstream rebrand was dead. He had alienated his creator base without satisfying the investors he was trying to attract. It was the worst possible outcome. A move designed to maximize value that destroyed value instead. He had tried to play both sides of the table and ended up losing at both. And then rather than rebuilding trust or rather than investing in the infrastructure that would make the platform resilient, he kept pulling dividends. $71 million in 2024 alone. Not reinvested, not deployed into compliance systems or creator relations or payment processor negotiations extracted, taken off the table as if the business were a savings account he was draining before closing it. The bill finally came due in 2025. By May of that year, Redvinsky was openly shopping the platform. The asking price was $8 billion. And to be fair to him, the number wasn't invented. It was anchored to real financials, $7.2 billion in gross revenue, $684 million in pre-tax profit, 80% market share in paid creator content. On paper, you could look at those numbers and argue the valuation was not just reasonable, but conservative. A healthy tech platform with those metrics and that market dominance should command a premium. But the buyers weren't stupid, and the buyers weren't just looking at the revenue line. The first serious conversation was with Forest Road Company, a Los Angeles-based media investment firm with experience in entertainment and digital media. Those talks went nowhere. The gap between what Radvinsky wanted and what the market believed the platform was actually worth. After the trust damage, after the regulatory exposure, after the payment processor relationships that kept threatening to suffocate the business, after the content liability lawsuits accumulating in the background was simply too wide to bridge. Forest Road looked at the real picture and walked away because the legal exposure wasn't theoretical anymore. It was in motion, no, and accelerating. OVCOM, the UK's media regulator, fined Phoenix Only Fans, roughly1 million pounds in early 2025 for failing to provide accurate data on age verification. That fine wasn't the damage. The fine was the signal. It told every other regulator in every other jurisdiction exactly where the vulnerabilities were. A Reuters investigation found that at least 128 people had filed complaints to US authorities about content on the platform. The legal exposure wasn't a cloud on the horizon anymore. It was weather you were already standing in. The $8 billion number started to look less like a valuation and more like a prayer. By January 2026, the number had collapsed. The new figure was $3.5 billion in equity with a total enterprise value of $5.5 billion when you factor in existing debt. And the counterparty sitting across the table wasn't a sovereign wealth fund or a blue chip private equity firm. It was Architect Capital, a San Francisco based asset lender that typically backs early stage startups and Latin American buy now pay later companies. That's who ends up with the keys to what was once supposed to be a 17 billion empire. Not SoftBank, not KKR, not a strategic acquirer with distribution and brand muscle. An assetbased lender known for distressed and emerging market positions. Music read that again. The platform that was supposed to IPO, the platform that generated more revenue than most publicly traded media companies, the platform that at its peak and processed more transactions than some regional banks, ended up in exclusive negotiations with an asset lender that usually backs fintech startups in Colombia and Brazil. That's not a sale. That's a liquidation wearing a suit. Let's talk about the math of greed because it's worth understanding precisely. Radinsky extracted over $1.8 $8 billion in dividends between 2021 and 2024. Every single dollar he pulled out was a dollar that did not go into compliance infrastructure. A dollar that did not go into content moderation systems capable of satisfying regulators. A dollar that did not go into rebuilding creator trust or strengthening the payment processor relationships that kept threatening to cut off the platform's oxygen supply. He wasn't investing in the business. He was harvesting it and sophisticated buyers can see that. Now, when a private equity firm or a serious strategic acquirer runs due diligence on a business, one of the first things they model is the reinvestment rate. How much of the earnings went back into building the platform versus how much was extracted. When that number skews as heavily as it did at Only Fans, it tells you something important. The business has been treated like a dividend vehicle, not a growth asset. The infrastructure is thinner than the revenue suggests. The moat is narrower than the market share implies. The PE multiple, the price buyers are willing to pay for each dollar of earnings, collapsed from the kind of premium tech company multiples Radvinsky was dreaming of, down to somewhere between three and five times Ebida. That's the floor. That's the multiple you get for distressed platforms in legally complex. They are reputationally damaged sectors. Not the ceiling of a growing tech company, the floor of an asset someone is buying at a discount because the previous owner bled it. He ran a cash machine like a piggy bank. And when he finally decided to sell, every buyer in the room could see exactly how much had been taken out, exactly what had been left behind, and exactly how hollow the infrastructure had become beneath the revenue figures. That gap between what a business earns and what its owner actually extracts versus reinvests is one of the most reliable early warning signals that a company's real value is being quietly destroyed while the surface numbers still look strong. It shows up before the regulatory fines, before the valuation collapse, before the failed sale process. It shows up in the dividend history, the capex ratios, the compliance headcount, the legal budget. If you know where to look, you can see it years in advance. And if you want to understand how to spot that pattern earlier, to read the signals before they become headlines. That's exactly the kind of analysis we break down in our newsletter every week. The link is in the description. The inside information is free. The regret of not reading it isn't. Only Fans isn't dead. Let's be clear about that. It still generates $7.2 billion in gross revenue. It still controls over 80% of the paid creator content market. The business model, 20% of every transaction with 80% going directly to creators, is still one of the most elegant margin structures anywhere on the internet. Simple, scalable, and brutally effective. For every dollar a fan spends, the platform keeps 20 cents and never has to touch the content, manage the talent, or own the inventory. That's not a business. That's a toll booth on a highway that builds itself. But the empire that could have been worth $17 billion dollars, a number that was floated in serious private conversations at the absolute height of pandemic growth, backed by real revenue trajectories and real market dominance, is now being sold at a fraction of that figure to a buyer that most people working in finance had genuinely never heard of before this deal was announced. Not a strategic acquirer, not a sovereign wealth fund, not a household name in private equity, an asset lender, a distressed capital shop, the kind of buyer you end up with when every better option has already looked at your books and walked away. And here's what makes that so striking as the underlying business didn't collapse. The revenue is still there. The market share is still there. The transaction volume is still there. What collapsed was the multiple, the premium that investors are willing to pay on top of the earnings because they believe in where the business is going. that premium evaporated because premium valuations require trust. They require the belief that the people running the asset are building something, not harvesting it. That belief was gone. And without it, even genuinely strong fundamentals get repriced at distressed asset levels. The creator community that built the thing still there, still posting, still paying the 20%, still functioning as the engine that drives every dollar of that $7.2 billion. Nothing about their behavior changed. They kept showing up. They kept producing. They kept growing their subscriber counts and delivering the content that made the platform indispensable to its users. The engine never stopped running. But the trust between creator and platform, that invisible social contract that made Only Fans genuinely different from every other adult content platform in history, was broken on August 19th, 2021. And it was never fully repaired. Not because the reversal didn't happen. It did. Not because the creators didn't come back. They did. But because everyone now understood the actual order of priorities, when it came down to a choice between creator welfare and institutional optics, Rvinsky chose institutional optics without hesitation. He reversed it when the cost was too high. But the choice itself had already been made. You don't forget that you adapt to it. You know, you build contingencies around it. You stop being loyal to something that showed you exactly how disposable you are the moment the math changes. That shift from loyal creator to hedging creator is invisible in the revenue numbers, but it's real and it's permanent. The banks didn't destroy Only Fans. The regulators didn't destroy Only Fans. The stigma of the adult content industry, the thing that mainstream media always assumed would be the platform's fatal vulnerability, didn't destroy Only Fans. None of the external forces that were supposed to bring it down, actually did. one man's decision to sacrifice long-term platform integrity for short-term institutional optics and then continue extracting hundreds of millions in dividends every single year while the damage quietly compounded beneath the surface did not dramatically not in a single moment slowly. The way rot works from the inside invisibly until the structure can no longer hold the weight it's supposed to carry. The lesson here isn't really about the adult content industry. It isn't about payment processors or UK regulators or content moderation policy. Those are the detail. The actual lesson is about what happens when the person who controls an asset, any asset in any industry, treats it like a personal ATM instead of a living business. A living business requires reinvestment. It requires trust maintenance. It requires the willingness to occasionally forgo short-term extraction in exchange for long-term compounding. A personal ATM just requires a pin code and a withdrawal limit you never quite respect. Fred Vinsky had one of the most defensible market positions in the history of digital media. Network effects so strong that competitors couldn't dislodge him even when they tried. A creator base so embedded that the switching costs were genuinely high. A payment model so clean it was almost self- sustaining. He had the rare combination of structural moat, revenue scale, and brand recognition that most founders spend their entire careers trying to build. He had all of that, and he chose the ATM. The content was always good. The creators were always there. The market was always real. The platform's potential that the $17 billion version of the story, the IPO version, the institutional capital version, was never fantasy. It was the natural destination for a business with those fundamentals. So, managed with even a modest amount of long-term discipline. The greed was the only thing that was optional. And in the end, the greed was the only thing that mattered.

  6. @ExposingUFOs: Karma is just Reddit’s social credit system. There’s upvotes and downvotes. 
People upvote what they like/agree with and downvote what they dislike/disagree with.

Your comment Karma is your total amount of upvotes minus your total amount of downvotes on comments.
Similarly, post Karma is upvotes minus downvotes on posts specifically.

Your Karma goes up & down accordingly. Karma is not static. 
It does go down if you get downvoted.

- how to be invisible online: Kind: captions Language: en So, you want to be untraceable online, completely invisible, a digital ghost, no small feat to accomplish. Maybe you watched Mr. Robot once and now you're ready to outwit the NSA, the FBI, and every bored data broker with a Wi-Fi connection. Well, buckle up because the only foolproof way to be 100% untraceable is to do exactly what this wise Redditor suggested. Turn off your computer and go outside. Of course, that kind of defeats the purpose, doesn't it? We like being online. We just don't like being tracked, profiled, and watched like a hawk. So, today, let's talk about what being untraceable really means, and whether you can actually pull it off, especially here in the good old United States of Surveillance. Spoiler, completely untraceable? Probably not. But difficult to trace? All right, that, my friends, is a game that we can play. And play it we shall. First, what does untraceable even mean? Are we asking for some mythical spell? When people say untraceable, they usually mean that nothing that they do online can be traced back to them and their real identity. No breadcrumbs, no data crumbs, nada. In an ideal scenario, you're a ghost. You post, you browse, you lurk, my favorite, and no one, not your ISP, not Google, not even the NSA's top secret Utah database, can figure out who, what, or where you are. Excuse me. It's ma'am. It is ma'am. Ma'am, once again, ma'am. This here is the NSA's Utah data center. It's a giant fortress in the Utah desert that allegedly allegedly stores unimaginable amounts of intercepted data. Yes, your tax dollars paid for a $ 1.5 billion facility to collect all the things. Being untraceable means that even facilities like these come up blank when they look for you. Ambitious, isn't it? Now, to be clear, there's a difference between privacy and anonymity. If you just want to keep your stuff private, then you want to control who has access to your data. Anonymity is just outright hiding who you are, like me. But keep in mind that whatever your end goal here is, if you're trying to hide from advertisers and nosy companies, or if you're trying to dodge the CIA, the FBI, the NSA, the PTA, and every other three-letter agency, the steps are going to differ a lot depending on who's after you. Hopefully, no one's after you. No one's after me, right? If you're just sick of personalized ads tracking your shoe size, relax. You don't need to launch yourself into full fugitive mode. But if you're aiming to be a internet ninja that even the NSA can't catch, well, in the US, that's like trying to win a game against someone with a thermal drone and a pack of blood hounds. In other words, it's tough. Fun fact, in leaked rules for the Xkey score program, the NSA literally flags people who just visit privacy websites, like you searched for tour or read about Linux and boom, you're marked as an extremist. All for being curious about privacy. So in the US, if you try too hard to be anonymous, you ironically stick out even more. It's the old wear a mask in a bank problem. Sure, it hides your face, but now everyone's watching the guy in a mask. Speaking of which, is the NSA watching me now? More H. All right. What kind of security solutions are available? So, let's say you're undeterred by everything I just said. You're going full Batman Gotham mode. No pair, no traces. What do you do? Well, you use a layered approach because there's no single magic tool that makes you invisible. Think of it kind of like an onion. We love onion analogies. Multiple layers of protection. It all comes full circle back to onions. I swear cyber security, it's just an onion. So, hardware and network hygiene. Your device itself can give you away. Ideally, use a burner device. There's a reason they throw these in movies. Something bought with cash or crypto if you can manage that. Not linked to you. Maybe a laptop from a pawn shop or a cheap phone from a gas station. Paid in green paper. No receipts. And if you get a receipt, burn it. Because if your laptop's serial number or your phone's IMEI is tied to you in any way, that's a direct trace. Next, your MAC address. That's a unique ID. Your Wi-Fi chip broadcasts to routers. By default, it's shouting, "Hey, look, it's John's MacBook." every time you connect, so it's best to randomize it. Now, modern operating systems have tools or settings where you can randomize or rotate your MAC address periodically. Now, it won't stop a nation state actor, but it stops the coffee shop Wi-Fi from recognizing your device ID. Also, pro tip, don't name your device John Doe's iPhone or Susan's PC. Yes, people do that. Rename it to something generic or just gibberish. Every little bit helps. If an investigator sees logs of a device named FBI surveillance car number four, actually that might be pretty funny, but you get my point. If you can consider a dedicated router or a special gadget that forces all your traffic through an immity networks by default, there are projects like the tour routers you can build on a Raspberry Pi called a tour box that will funnel everything through tour. That way, even your toaster and your fridge are accidentally anonymous because who knows, your fridge might be telling the NSA who you are. Now, outside of hardware, let's talk about operating systems. Running Windows 11 with your named user account while trying to remain untraceable is like if I wore a name tag right now saying my full name. What would be the point of wearing the mask? Instead, you want a privacy focused operating system, something like Tails or Hunix. Hunenix is a Linux-based operating system specifically designed for anonymity. It actually runs as two virtual machines. One routes all your network traffic through tour and the other is where you do your stuff. The workstation only talks to the internet through the tour gateway. This setup means that even if you screw up and some app tries to reveal your IP, it can't because by default it's trapped behind a tour wall. And Hunix comes with a lot of privacy tools right out the box for browser safe defaults stuff like that. Now Tails is another option. It's a live operating system that you boot off of a USB stick. Forces all traffic over to as well. And when you shut it down, it leaves no traces on the host machine. Basically, it's like Mission Impossible, but with a self-destructing operating system. No logs left behind. It's a very nice. Now, a seasoned privacy guru will tell you that even operating systems can only go so far. They help a lot, but if you misconfigure something or reveal info in other ways, the jig is up. Still, if you're trying to be a ghost, using a purpose-built operating system is kind of a no-brainer. Your IP address is one of the biggest breadcrumbs. It's like your house address on the internet. If you use your home IP, it's game over immediately. That's tied to your ISP account and by extension you. So, you need to mask your IP. Get it? So, what are your options? VPNs, tour, and proxies or even chaining them together. VPNs. Most of you know about this one. It routes your traffic through a server run by a VPN provider. The website see the VPN's IP, not yours. But here's the catch. You're basically handing all your internet traffic over to the VPN provider, so you better trust them. Most good VPNs will claim the no logs policy, which means that they swear that they don't record what you do. But we have to take their word for it. And some have been caught lying. And also, pro tip, I didn't know about this until I looked it up. If a VPN is headquartered in a country that's a part of the Five Eyes, sounds like a Naruto reference, I know. The Five Eyes Intelligence Alliance, the US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, or other surveillanceheavy jurisdictions. Think twice about that VPN. Law enforcement and intelligence can compel companies in these countries to hand over data or even start logging. For instance, the NSA and its buddies have legal authority to force force even no logVPNs to cough up info. On the other hand, some VPN providers have proven their no log policy in court, and I'm not going to name Jordan specifically, but a certainish VPN, that was my attempt at a Swedish accent, was raided by the police and they had to leave because they straight up had nothing. It was gone. Look for VPNs like that. So, if you go the VPN route, pick a provider known for privacy and ideally in a privacyfriendly place. Switzerland, anyone? Make sure that you pay anonymously. Don't just use your credit card. Most good VPNs these days will take gift cards or crypto. And don't use your personal email to register. Common sense, folks. That way, your account isn't obviously linked to you. Tour. This is the big one. Tour is a free network that bounces your traffic through multiple volunteerrun nodes, servers all over the world. Like taking a very convoluted route to a website. Connection goes through, say, three nodes, each encrypted in layers, hence the onion name. The entry node knows you. The exit node knows what site you're accessing but not who you are. No single node knows the whole chain. It's pretty freaking awesome. And it gives strong anonymity at the cost of speed. Tour is slow compared to the normal internet. You're certainly not going to be gaming on that or streaming 4K through tour. Forget about it. But for web browsing, chatting or accessing onion sites, it's the gold standard for anonymity. And the tour browser is what most people use. But in some countries, tour is outright blocked or even illegal. We see China and Russia. Thankfully, in the US, tour is legal. Just maybe don't brag about it on Facebook with your real name. For maximum security, the hardcore folks will combine VPNs and tour and proxies in various orders like connect to a VPN, then launch tour browser, so even your ISP only sees VPN traffic, but can't see what you do on tour. There are also fancy setups like running tour inside of a VPN inside of another VPN. At some point, the chain might get ridiculous and very slow, but it can add resistance against cyber attacks and tracking. Just be careful, though, because combining tools can sometimes backfire if you do it wrong. Example, misconfigured VPNs could leak your IP while you thought you were hidden. Are you managing multiple accounts and getting flagged because they're all coming from your lonely single home or business IP address? Now, there is a thing called browser and app compartmentalization. Try saying that three times fast. Imagine you've got an anonymous operating system running on a burner laptop behind a VPN and tour. You're practically a secret agent now, but you open up a browser and you log into a personal Gmail or Facebook account. You just linked back to your real identity. Opsac fail. OBSE is a huge part of all this. You must compartmentalize your online activities. Separate the persona that's anonymous from the one that's you in real life. This means no logging into personal accounts on your ghost setup. Create new email accounts, preferably with something like Proton Mail or Tuta, formerly known as Tutonota, that does not require your real info. Use encrypted messaging like Signal for communications. I use Signal personally. And again, use a new phone number for setups like that or a secondary phone that's not actually your real cell phone number. For each anonymous persona that you operate, use unique usernames and passwords that you've never used before anywhere else and even consider your writing style if you want to be extra careful. Believe it or not, this was news to me as well at some point. People can be identified by how they write called styometry. You always spell a certain word wrong or say something like okie dokie. That could link your alias back to you. Neat, huh? Some experts say that to truly be able to do a different persona, you practically need to have a split personality disorder, like me. They were joking, I think. But but the point stands. You need to mentally separate yourself from your real life and your anonymous life. No crossover. That means if your real name is John and you love posting about football on your personal Twitter, your anonymous handle should not be ranting about the same game with the same writing style on the same forum. You'd be surprised by how little it takes for investigators to connect the dots. Here's a cool new modern approach. Use browser isolation or cloud containers for extra safety. Instead of browsing directly from your home machine, you can spin up a temporary web browser in the cloud. Tools like Chasm Workspaces can provide a disposable browser on a remote server. Think of it like a throwaway desktop in the cloud. You can do all your sketchy or privacy conscious browsing there. If any malware tries to infect you, it infects the container and not the machine. And when you're done, destroy the container. Poof, all gone. Now, the remote server IP is what gets seen by websites and not yours. Similarly, you can rent out a cheap virtual private network under an alias and use it as a relay or for browsing. Essentially, it's like your own personal VPN node that you control. Just be cognizant that paying for a VPS service usually leaves a trail. Again, try using crypto or gift cards and register with an email alias. The usual stuff. Encryption everywhere. Encrypt all the things. Make sure that all your communication is encrypted. Use HTTPS only, which is a standard now for websites anyways. Use end-to-end encrypted chat, Signal, WhatsApp, and make sure you make note of what's tied to a phone number like WhatsApp or truly anonymous chat, perhaps something like session, which is a fork of Signal that can work without phone numbers or XMPPP with OMO. If you're emailing, use PGP or a service that does automatic end-to-end encryption like Proton Mail. That way, if even someone intercepts your message, all they get is cipher text that they can't read. But also mind your metadata. Even if content is encrypted, who you talk to, when and how long gets leaked. A former CIA NSA director famously said, "We kill people based on metadata." He meant in counterterrorism, of course, but it highlights that metadata can reveal almost anything about your habits and your relationships without even having to see the message. So try to minimize metadata. Use tor win messaging. Send messages at random intervals, not Monday at 8 a.m. on the clock. This is spy level stuff, so decide for yourself how far down the rabbit hole you need to go. But in the end of it all, it always comes back to behaviors. Even the strongest privacy setup can be defeated by one oopsy daisy, like using your secure alias account from your home Wi-Fi just once or mentioning your hometown in an anonymous forums post. OBPS is a lifestyle, not a one-time install. It requires constant vigilance. Think of all the high-profile hackers and black market operators who got caught almost always. It wasn't their encryption that was failing. It was human error. Boy, ain't that the truth with cyber security. For example, the founder of the Silk Road marketplace, Russ Ulich, went to extreme lengths to hide his online drug empire through tour. Yet he got caught because he reused an online username from an old forum and even posted his personal email in the early days of Silk Road. One tiny lapse and it gives investigators enough of a lead to piece things together until the whole thing is unraveled and boom goes the dynamite. And you will make mistakes. Being anonymous is hard. As one online commenter put it, "You'll spend years working towards the goal, make plenty of mistakes you might not even realize it, yet so good to the point that the CIA or the NSA could hire you. If they never give you a call, maybe you succeeded, and if they do knock at your door, well, point proven. Being completely untraceable was a tad bit unrealistic." By now, you might be thinking, "Gesh, this sounds like a full-time job. Bingo." Achieving near anonymity is a part-time job at minimum and a full-on lifestyle at the most. For an average person, it's probably overkill to do everything. But there's good news. You don't have to be 100% untraceable to greatly improve your privacy and your security. Just doing a few things like VPN or tour or proxy for sensitive browsing, using encrypted communication methods, disabling invasive trackers, stuff like that will put you miles ahead of the average internet user. The goal is to ultimately reduce your digital footprint, make tracking hard, and protect yourselves from the easy grabs of data out there, and our data is getting leaked a lot. You may not have to evade a determined national intelligence agency, but you can absolutely evade script kitty hackers, nosy co-workers, or data- hungry corporations. And if I may be frank, that covers 99% of threats that people actually face. In the US, we do have an uphill battle with surveillance. The legal framework often favors law enforcement or intel agencies over personal privacy. The Patriot Act of 2001, for example, authorized unprecedented spying on Americans. And folks worldwide, even though some parts of it did expire, the surveillance infrastructure it built still exists. Companies too track the hell out of us, building profiles from our search queries, purchases, Facebook likes, social media interactions. But luckily, public awareness and the demand for privacy is growing. Encryption is more mainstream now. VPNs are advertised everywhere. I'm guilty of that one. There's a push for privacy laws. And if you're aiming to work in cyber security, tinkering with these anonymity tools is actually a great way to learn. You'll understand how networks and systems actually work. And you'll develop a security mindset, or as I like to call it, an overly anxious, paranoid mindset. Setting up Hunix, practicing OPSEC, using tour. That's hands-on experience that employers will value and at the very least will show that you're passionate about the field. Just maybe don't tell your job interviewer that you spent your weekends trying to be untraceable because you're hiding from the NSA unless you phrase it very carefully. So, is it possible to be completely untraceable in the United States? The honest answer, effectively untraceable, maybe if you're extremely disciplined, lucky, and you only face moderate adversaries. Completely untraceable against a US government investigation. Probably not with the current tech. And there's an old saying, the only true secure system is one that is powered off, cast in a block of concrete, and sealed in a leadlined room with armed guards. And even then, I have my doubts. Now, instead of chasing perfection, aim for progress. Use the tools as practices at your disposal to reduce your traceability. Be aware of the information that you give out publicly and freely, and prove the privacy where you can. Think of it kind of like healthy eating. You might not become a chiseled Olympian, but every cheeseburger that you don't eat helps. In the same way, every bit of data that you don't give out to marketers and spies is a win for you and frankly a win for society. The more average people that use encryption and privacy tools, the harder it is for mass surveillance to target everyone. So go forth, my privacy padawans, with realistic expectations in hand. You probably won't vanish like a puff of smoke in the digital world, but you can become a lot harder to follow. And in a world where everyone's info is up for grabs, that's something to be proud of. - a Practical Guide To Online Privacy in 2026: Kind: captions Language: en Your computer is a worse snitch than your ex. It logs your behavior, your browsing, your habits, and then quietly hands that data to companies you've never heard of. Not because you messed up, because that's the default. I typed ipconfig/displaydns last week and my computer vomited every website I visited Amazon, YouTube, that weird 3:00 a.m. CODC review site just sitting there for Google to read, for my ISP to sell, for anyone with log access to see. 2026 is going to be brutal for privacy. AI scraping everything. ISPs legally required to log your activity. every app selling your location to data brokers who sell it to insurance companies who deny your claims because you visited too many burger joints. But your privacy is actually fixable today for free. And I spent 3 months down this rabbit hole after my smart TV was recording my conversations and sending them to advertisers. When you type netflix.com, your computer asks a DNS server where Netflix lives. Problem is, your ISP runs those DNS servers by default. Every website you visit gets logged, kept for years, sold to advertisers. Your ISP knows more about you than your therapist. Fix is stupid simple. Cloudflare Warp free app, 30 seconds to install. Routes DNS queries through Cloudflare instead of your ISP. So your ISP sees you're making requests but not what you're actually visiting. Like watching someone mail letters but can't read what's inside. And it makes your internet faster. Sounds like scam, but it's real. My DNS queries went from 45 milliseconds to 12. four times faster for free while protecting privacy. Cloudflare makes money from enterprise services. They offer this free as loss leader, not a VPN replacement. If you need to pretend you're in Switzerland, you still need VPN. But for normal people who don't want their ISP building profiles, Cloudflare Warp is perfect. Takes 2 minutes. Do it now. Four free tools from Microsoft that show you exactly what's running. TCP view shows every network connection in real time. Right now I see Firefox, Discord, then something called MSMPG. Turns out it's Windows Defender calling home to Microsoft with my system data. If you've got malware, cryptominers, sketchy free software, TCP view shows it, every process making network calls, where it's connecting. You can kill it right there. Process explorer is task manager on steroids. Shows every process running. Configure it to check every executable against virus total. Scans with 60 different anti virus engines. If you've got malware, you'll see red marks everywhere. Auto runs shows everything that starts automatically when you boot. Services, drivers, schedule tasks, everything. You can just uncheck things. Don't want Nvidia spying on GPU usage? Uncheck it. Done. Process monitor is nuclear option. Shows everything happening in real time. Files opening, registry modifications, network connections. Set up filters like only show network activity from unknown processes. Spend an afternoon learning these four tools. Worth it. If you're using Chrome in 2026, you're installing spyware. You are the product. Firefox is the alternative. Not perfect, but at least not actively destroying ad blockers. Google introduced Manifest v3, which breaks ad blockers. Every Chromium browser, Chrome, Edge, Brave, Opera has to follow Google's rules. Firefox doesn't. When Google kills ad blockers, Firefox keeps working. Two extensions minimum. Bit Warden, Password Manager. free open- source. If you're reusing passwords, you're one breach away from losing everything. Bit warden generates random passwords for every site, stores encrypted, syncs across devices. When site gets breached, and they all do, damage is contained. Hackers get one useless password. Second extension, you block origin blocks ads. More importantly, blocks tracking. Every ad online is tracking you, building profile, watching where you go, what you click, all to serve more ads. Zero trust is simple. Assume everything is compromised until proven otherwise. Link from co-orker. Verify it's them. Software download. Check signature. Email from bank. Go to their website directly. Friend asking for money on Instagram. Call them. Actually call them. Attackers use your trust against you. They know you'll click link from friend. They know you'll panic at urgent suspicious activity. They exploit human psychology. Only defense is assuming everything is fake until you verify it's real. Perfect privacy doesn't exist unless you're in cabin in Montana. But you can make yourself harder target. Force data brokers and advertisers and ISPs to work for it instead of handing it to them free. None of this is hard. None costs money. Only barrier is caring enough to do it. Most people won't. They'll keep using Chrome with no ad blocker, typing passwords manually, clicking every link, wondering why they keep getting hacked. Don't be most people. Take 2 hours set this up. Your future self will thank you when you're not dealing with identity theft or ransomware. See you next one. If you like the way I break this stuff down, then check out Cyberflows Academy, my private community. Inside you get full step-by-step courses on ethical hacking, bug bounty, web hacking, osent, Python, C++, and reverse engineering, plus a private Discord where you can ask me anything and learn alongside people already getting insane payouts. You also get all my cyber docs, the cheat sheets, workflows, recon templates, osent checklists, exploit notes, and practical challengebased labs that actually force you to apply the skills. And we include all my tools and setups so your machine is ready from day one. If you want all that, courses, labs, playbooks, everything, it's in the first link in the description. See you inside.

ratio of the photo that you're working with this is going to change now what if i wanted to have a little bit more of a freehand adjustment where i could totally change the shape and dimensions of my - How To Crop an Image in Photoshop With Specific Sizes: Kind: captions Language: en To get to the crop tool in Photoshop, you want to go to the fifth tool down and then left click in here. Now, if you don't see this available, you can right click and it'll be the very top one. Once you've got yourself the crop tool selected, you will notice first of all, you will have this border on your image. You can make the height of it much more smaller or you can reduce both of them at the same time by dragging the corners. And as you can see, we now have this image which we can easily crop to only focus on the person. This tool has some really important options right at the top. So, we're going to start on the very first one, which is the ratio and resolution. So, the first one allows you to set a specific or custom ratio. For example, if you wanted a one by one square, you can just set it to this right here. And what this will do is it will lock in the aspect ratio. So no matter how hard you try to change the height, it will lock it in and it will just maintain the ratio of it. We have the ratio and we have the resolution. So this is if you wanted to be more specific with your image. You can set it to the resolution and you'll have the option for the width and the height or you can just set it to a specific resolution and it will lock it in at that aspect ratio. So first of all you would simply select yourself pixel/in or pixel/ cm. So let's say for example we wanted this to be a,000 pixels. We would just simply type it in here and this will make it 1,000 * 1,000. Now, let's say that you had a specific measurement in mind and you wanted it something like 1280 * 720 right in here. You would type in the width and the height in these two boxes right here. You can also click on the clear button right here and this will just reset it back to normal. And if you want to, you can just set it on the original ratio, and that will reset it back to normal. All you have to do now is crop this image down to what you prefer. And you also want to make sure that you have this button ticked, which will say delete cropped pixels. What this will basically do is it will delete anything outside of this box. The very last three options, which is reset, cancel, and apply. And you want to go ahead and click on the tick icon to apply the changes. And there you go. So, as you can see, we have now cropped this image down. And if you unlock this to be a layer and press Ctrl and T, you can now see that this is a standalone image and we've fully cropped it down. And that is pretty much it. That's how you crop an image in Photoshop.

- How to Crop an Image in PowerPoint: Kind: captions Language: en PowerPoint has the option to crop images inside of a presentation so that you don't need a separate app to select the part of the image that matters. Let's learn how. In PowerPoint, I've got a presentation here where I'll paste an image into my presentation. I like the image that I'm working with here, but I'd like to crop it so that only part of it is showing. With the image selected, click on the picture tools format menu option. To start cropping, I'll click on the crop option here on the ribbon. Once you've selected to crop an image, you can add these black bars that appear on the sides and corners of the image. Crop an image by grabbing one of these handles and dragging it so that only the pixels we want to keep are remaining. You'll see PowerPoint dim out the pixels that won't be used. To lock in the crop, I'll just click crop on the ribbon again. Now the unused pixels are removed. We could also reenter the crop options by clicking on it again and resize the crop area to include pixels we had already removed. Cropping is a breeze in PowerPoint, so don't open up Photoshop or another app the next time you need to do it. I'm Andrew Childress for Touch Plus, and thanks for joining me.
