- How To Get an Anonymous Bank Account in 2025: Kind: captions Language: en In today's surveillance obsessed world, is it still possible to open truly anonymous accounts in first tier banks? We're not talking about bise banana republics nor payment platforms. I'm talking about high street banks in Europe, in the United States, and in Asia. The ones you thought were completely off limits. Let's find out. This is episode seven of the poor man's Pablo Escobar series where we dive into the underworld of offshore finances and give our own commentary. And in today's case, we have the popular tape brothers who tried opening anonymous accounts using a fake Mexican passport with a James Bond alias. Turns out millions can't buy you common sense. But the truth is that anonymous accounts absolutely exist only under the Russian way. And in the next few minutes, I'm going to break down how and why the Russian method works, the one trait that Westerners need to actually pull it off, and of course, a step-by-step breakdown on how you can do it yourself without getting flagged. Let's dive straight into it. Anonymous bank accounts in first tier countries with real compliance still work, but only if you have one thing, a reliable front. The frontman is the one who acts as the official owner and manager of the bank account. They're the ones who are going to sign those banking documents, the tax forms, and be part of the company registries. Meanwhile, you, the ultimate beneficial owner, stay in the shadows. And for that service, of course, you pay them a generous salary. Now, you would think that with all of the millions that the Tates have, Tristan Tate would just be able to buy a front man, right? I mean, why go through faking a Mexican passport and adding a bunch of criminal charges out of thin air? Because they can't trust anybody in Western society. That is extremely tough to do. Other than his other notorious brother, there's nobody else to rely upon. Western society is extremely commercial and contractual, which is great for business, but terrible for building relationships and loyalty because handing over your assets to someone else with a silent agreement requires deep mutual trust. Because the frontmen can literally walk away with all of your money, but at the same time, they're legally responsible for all the stuff that you're doing behind the scenes. And that's why it works. Both parties know and trust each other very well. They understand that each party has the other's fate in their hands. And paradoxically, that's what makes it work. Latin Americans, a lot of Eastern Europeans and Middle Easterners are raised this way from a very early age. They have a code of conduct and they have these strong tribal and family bonds throughout their entire lives. In contrast, the western norm is you only spend time with your family twice per year during Thanksgiving and Christmas and you only call them to ask for money. But some of the best nominees that the easterners use are their family members. And it only works if you have a good relationship with your parents and siblings. One of the best examples I can give you is the Moroccan football player Hakeimi. When his ex gold digging wife tried taking half of his after the divorce, she found out that he owed nothing. Everything was in his mother's name, including his PSG salary. And by the way, this structure, it also works in Western countries. We'll dive a bit into the details later on. Now, just because the strategy works does not mean it's easy to pull off today, especially in first tier countries. And that's why the Russian method is getting more expensive. Used to be cheap. Just found a forest gump off the streets, you paid him pennies, and you opened a bank account in his name. But with modern-day banking compliance, that's pretty much dead. Today, most of those accounts are opened in a company's name. And during the corporate account opening process, your nominee, the frontman, gets interviewed. And he's asked questions like, "What's the company's business? What's the purpose of the account? Why specifically this jurisdiction? And what's the plan for the future funds? And he has to be sharp and answer convincingly. His story needs to make sense from the bank's perspective. And most importantly, he needs to have a passport that checks out. In modern banking, presentation matters. And the smarter and more coherent your front man is with the right passport, banks really, really like that. And of course, that's why they get paid a premium. In lower tier countries like Turkey, you can still get away with dragging someone off the street to see the bank manager. In fact, that's what a lot of OTC dealers do to trade cryptos not in their name. But when it comes to Switzerland, Likensstein, or the UK, forget about it. That's why Turkish businesses dealing with international banks will actually find ethnic northern criates who hold an EU cyprress passport to be their front man. And Colombians do the same thing. They have a bunch of aunts and uncles in the UK or the US who will receive decent pensions into their bank accounts. And after the 2014 Ukraine war, Russians suddenly seeing their passports discriminated did pretty much the same thing. They went to the Donbass region in the Ukraine where there are a bunch of ethnic Russians with Ukrainian passports and used those guys as frontmen which the Swiss banks were far more comfortable dealing with. Now let me break a common myth here. The Russian way is not the same as those cheap nominee services that are usually bundled up in these offshore company packages where you have a Panameanian guy or a criate who signs off as a director or a shareholder on paper, but they also make you sign a bunch of other documents that protect them and limit their legal liability. And those other documents such as the deed of trust, the POA, by their very nature means that the nominee is not really the person in charge. They're just there to hide your name from the public registries. Because by signing off as the ultimate beneficial owner, they're the ones that take all of the legal responsibility for your actions. And no same person is going to sign off on all those bank account forms or the payment platforms for a guy that they don't even know. You could be a international drug trafficker and they would be the ones that take the hit. And by the way, this stuff happens a lot. So if somebody ever offers you a lot of money to be the person signing off on all these documents, don't do it. Your freedom is far more valuable than some quick buck that you're going to make. And another misconception is that trusts will not make you anonymous when it comes to banking. Especially in Switzerland or Likenstein, they will ask who created the trust and also all the beneficiary names. If you want to open an anonymous account for your trust, even the Rothschilds, they still had to use a frontman named Kodorovski, as I explain in more detail in this video. So, how do you, as a Westerner, apply all of this? Is there still a way for you to get these accounts? And the answer is absolutely, as long as you have reliable family members. In the case of Hakeime, he had his mother, and that's the best asset protection mechanism for you. But you want to make sure that you have a structure where it doesn't require your family members to be financial geniuses. And you can even use this structure to move your cryptos into bank accounts and hide their ties to you from prying eyes. Step number one, start small. Startups and small businesses have an easy time opening bank accounts and EMI accounts, but it must be something that matches Craig's skill set. You want to open a corporate payment platform account with Revolute, Mercury, Airwallix, whatever matches the jurisdiction. And that's where you're going to be sending the first initial deposit into the bank account from. And then step number two, you want to make sure that the business that Craig runs actually matches what he knows, the backstory he needs to check out because otherwise the banks, they'll sniff him out very quickly. You don't make an exelect electrician the CEO of a crypto trading platform. You want something that you can train him to speak about confidently. And from the bank's perspective, it makes perfect sense why he would be upgrading to a bank account because payment platforms are riskier for holding money there long term. Step number three, train the nominee. Spend a few days and go over the business plan with him. You want to make sure that Craig is comfortable with it. You can go with chat GPT or even read the E-Myth highly recommended book to build the operational framework for the company. You will however need contracts, agreements, invoices and apostled financial statements to present to the bank. You can check my previous videos for more ideas on that front. And then step number four is the actual bank interview. Now during the interview, the bank manager, they will have four questions in mind. First, why does Craig's business need a bank account in this particular jurisdiction and in this particular bank? Then secondly, how does the business actually operate and where does it make its money? Third, what are going to be the inflows into the bank account? And then fourth, how are you planning on using that money afterwards? Pro tip, tell them that you want to keep it in the bank long term. They obviously love it because the bank makes more money that way. However, here's a key point. You will not accompany him to the interview. He does it all by himself because otherwise that defeats the whole purpose. The bank manager is going to say, "Okay, well, what's your ties to the business?" You want to stay invisible. Step number four and a half, banks log where the accounts are accessed from. So Craig, he needs to register and access the bank account from devices and IPs that you can securely reach. And this all has to be built into the company's backstory, especially if you're living abroad. If not, your accounts can quickly get flagged. And then step number five, once the account has already been opened, the hard part is over. You can now start moving money inside. Again, depending on what the backstory was. If it was a mature operation, you can plow in larger amounts. But if you started off with a startup, you're going to have to do it slowly through a trading company tied to the business. and do it step by step with flexible accounting. Now, that was a brief overview. Obviously, there's more detail that goes into every single step. But let's finish off on a practical note. Do not waste your time or money on these ready-made shelf companies with bank accounts. They're usually advertised on online forums. It's a company that's set up by somebody else with an active account. Then they transfer the ownership to you without informing the bank that the company has switched hands and you're just expected to act as if nothing ever happened and use it. history to protect their nest eggs from corrupt governments or illegal seizures or even confiscation in their home countries but let's start looking at exactly what is an anonymous bank account an anonymous account also referred to as a numbered account is a bank account where the identity of the account holder is replaced with a multi-digit number or code the name of the client is only accessible to the client and a small circle of senior staff at the bank traditionally the account holder's name and code number were written on an index card stashed away in the bank safe and was only accessible to the banker and their boss like any account the money could either sit there and do nothing or it could be invested and grow it was up to the client but here's where things started to get dodgy it was the account holder's responsibility to report the account to the appropriate tax authorities if the owner didn't declare the account it was nearly impossible for tax authorities to find out that it existed in many cases this meant that the owner wouldn't pay taxes on the money in that anonymous account basically in the era of banking secrecy the thinking was that if no one knew about your money it wasn't taxable well that era is over everything changed in 2007. that's when ubs got busted for helping americans evade taxes with numbered accounts not surprisingly the u.s retaliated and rolled out the big guns it took three years for foreign account tax compliance act or fatca to be introduced that was quickly followed by a similar effort from the oecd in 2014 which you know as the common reporting standards or crs all of this was then followed by the panama papers leak in 2016 which then helped to expedite global information sharing governments everywhere cried out for more transparency more compliance more tax revenue so today we have tougher banking laws beefier kyc and aml rules and new compliance standards across the board translation banking secrecy is debt and for predetermined guilt which is a fancy way of saying your money is frozen by the court because they think you're guilty before trial finally taxes let's not be naive one of the main reasons for wanting For instance, if you're running a facts channel, you could go for using only stock footage. And if you need these kinds of images or videos, here are a few websites: Pixabay, Freepik, Mixkit, or Envato. Now, I'd say I use stock photos or videos about 10% of the time for my videos. In most cases, I combine animations with effects and texts. It takes a bit of time to get the right feel for the effects and the video. Now, you'll understand why it's also important to write the scripts in parts. I work with phrases and try to find the perfect animation and video for each. Let's take this phrase as an example. I talk about ChatGPT and CapCut, so I made this animation that perfectly illustrates what the voiceover is saying. Now, I'm going to teach you how easily I put it together. You choose a text, import the icons or images you want to talk about, choose to fade in as an animation, and you're done. It's that simple. Now, you could repeat the same thing, but the video would get boring if you used the same technique every time. So, I'm going to share another one with you. And don't forget to smash the like button – this video cost me a lot in special effects. If you're stuck on editing, you can use words. CapCut has a bunch of text templates and styles you can use to make plain text look fantastic. But don't overuse this technique; it can take up a lot of your time. Just pick a sentence and try to sync it with the voiceover. It might sound complicated, but check out this example to understand it. Simple but effective. Match the words and go for short sentences. No need for fancy effects, just the text. My last tip on this topic: if you're struggling with an editing gap, focus on one word and try to implement an animation from Canva or stock footage that visualizes that word. Now, let's talk about backgrounds. I don't want to dwell too much on this topic. As you can see, I'm sticking to a single background because I believe that just having a black background doesn't look that appealing. I created mine using Canva, and in just a few seconds, I'll show you how I did it.So, we've covered scripting, animations, and backgrounds, and now the only things left are sound effects and music. There isn't much you need to pay attention to here. When you play music in the background, it's crucial to choose non-copyrighted music to avoid getting banned or receiving a copyright strike – YouTube's got your back on that. Additionally, I'd recommend adding sound effects to your transitions and some elements of the video. You might wonder why. Well, watch the same video without sound effects and then with sound effects. Notice the difference? Just be sure to add a sound after every transition. With a bit of practice, you'll get the hang of it. A compelling YouTube title and description are crucial for attracting viewers and improving search visibility, as they serve as the first point of engagement and key indicators for algorithms. Alright, let me walk you through how I pick my titles. First things first, I browse through videos in my niche on YouTube. I scroll through my YouTube feed until I stumble upon a catchy title that fits my video. Once I find one, I copy the title and head over to Chat GPT. I paste the title, use a specific command, and voila, I've got my title. After that, with another command, I get my description sorted, and there you have it – a nice description and a captivating title ready for your video upload. Easy as that! You sit there scrolling through comments with one hand, the other still resting where you posted, like your body hasn't caught up to the fact that the performance is over. A notification pops, another subscriber, another tip, another message that sounds like admiration, but reads like ownership. You don't celebrate. You just refresh, because what people see is confidence, control, easy money. They assume you figured something out they haven't. What they don't see is how carefully everything is calculated. The lighting angle that hides the parts you don't like, the captions that feel casual but are rewritten three times, the way you monitor your own face while performing, like you're both the actor and the audience at the same time. From the outside, it looks like empowerment. From the inside, it can feel like constant exposure. This is where people get it wrong. They think the pressure comes from judgement, but often it comes from something quieter, self-objectification. It's a psychological concept where you start seeing yourself the way others see you, reducing your identity to how you appear, how you perform, how you're consumed. Not because you're weak, because the environment rewards it, and over time the line blurs. You stop asking, "How do I feel?" and start asking, "How do I look while feeling this?" There's a moment that makes this real. It's late, maybe 2:13 a.m. Your phone screen lights up your face in the dark. You reread a message that says, "You're perfect." And instead of feeling flattered, you instinctively check your last post, zoom in, tilt your head slightly, notice a flaw no one else mentioned. And just like that, the compliment turns into a correction. Here is the paradox. The more attention you receive, the harder it can become to feel seen. Because attention focuses on parts, but being seen requires wholeness. And when your income, validation, and routine are tied to how you're perceived, stepping away doesn't just feel like rest, it can feel like disappearing. But here's the quiet truth people don't talk about. You're allowed to exist outside the frame, outside the lighting, outside the version of you that performs well. There is a version of you that isn't being watched, read, or saved. And that version isn't less valuable. It's just unobserved. And sometimes that's where your mind finally exhales. Not everything you do has to be content. Not every moment needs to hold attention. Some things are allowed to be yours, unseen, unoptimized, real. So, do I just burn my entire life to the ground?" I mean, that's what I did, but I understand why some people wouldn't want to do that. So, yeah, I think that's another big reason why a lot of people just don't leave cuz they don't know what to do. And I think the whole world tells you that no one's going to want you now, no one's going to want to marry you, hire you, um that you have like no skills, and that your life is basically ruined, and you you made the worst decision. And I think that's what keeps people in the industry as well cuz they don't really have hope for anything else. Yeah. Can you share a bit about how stepping away from OnlyFans affected your life and your mental health? Yeah, I feel like it's just been a weight off my shoulders. I don't I feel like now I can fully just step into myself and just focus on myself. And I think a lot of at least my personal journey was that I just didn't believe in myself enough, and so I wanted that crutch of money. And, you know, because I didn't trust that what I felt called to do would actually work out, so it was kind of like a buffer. And I think now, you know, seeing that path completely just crash and burn, I'm like, "Okay, now I'm still stuck in my purpose and everything, so I must just go full force." So, I think it's also been a very like, you know, self-transformation journey. And I just think now I'm personally way more confident than I was years ago making that decision. So, that's also really helped. And yeah, I think just overall my mental I don't have to worry about this. I mean, I do have some lingering issues that I'm just like, "This is so stupid." But, you know? And I think now I feel very like empowered to talk about things. I think there's a lot of people in this industry who want me silent. Well, I know they want me silent cuz we were fighting. But, so I think that being able to just come out and talk about it, and, you know, even helping one girl who's thinking of like, "Maybe this is the path for me." Or, you know, I've had people reach out to me about how to get out of their contracts and stuff. I also work with like one of the most amazing lawyers ever who's very called to this mission as well. So, I think also doing that, I understand the purpose of that journey. And being able to come back with all of this information and really just share it with people, I think that's been like the best, and that's, you know, I don't know. It makes me almost like not grateful, but like a kind of grateful that it happened. So, yeah, I think my mental has been better, but no, I would never do that again. It's brutal. Yeah. And thank you for sharing that in that way. It's so important to hear from people like you who have experienced this because I think something you said at the beginning of that was just noting that you didn't believe in yourself enough. And this is really an industry that preys upon insecurity, right? It preys upon promising a better life if someone will do exactly, you know, what is spelled out for them. And I think it takes so much courage to be willing to look at that and confront that and say this maybe actually isn't what I want to be doing. And And then to be so bold as to share your story now to help others know what this really is like to go through this experience because this is something that's being so normalized and pitched to young people as uh a super easy, empowering, glamorous means to an end. And it's really not. I mean, yeah, that was a lot of my journey. Like I I think, you know, it's really hard when people are like, "Oh, money won't make you happy." And I think everyone says it, but until you really live the experience of having everything and feeling empty, it's kind of hard to trust when people tell you that. And I think that's really what I've learned through this as well is that all of the the glitz and the glamour is not going to make you happy if you are doing something that you hate and you are, you know, empty and lacking purpose. And so, that's also where the belief in myself has really come from is like, "Okay, if I've been called to do something, I need to trust that calling. I need to trust myself instead of running away and believing that a lifestyle is going to make it better and that money is going to make it better and that if I'm able to, you know, go to these places and do these things, that I'm going to feel better." Cuz you're going to do that and you're going to be miserable everywhere cuz you're not dealing with like the root of the problem. Yeah. It's so important to have stories like you were shared. Um so, again, I want to thank you for being willing to share yours with us. What inspired you to start speaking out and sharing your experience publicly? Um so, I first talked about it December 2024. I posted a TikTok video. And I don't know what it was. I mean, I honestly think it might have been when Lily Phillips first came out with that video. And just something about seeing it just sparked something in me and I was like, "Oh my god, I got to talk about this." And I was so nervous. Like I was so scared to talk about it. I was like, "Oh my god." Cuz I was still in the industry. And I was like, "Oh, this is going to be This is going to be brutal." And I was also still heavily involved in some legal matters. So, I was like, "Oh my god, this is just terrible." But I just felt the call on my heart, you know? And I was like, "Okay, if I'm feeling called to speak now, then I have to speak now." And I wrestled with that for a little bit. And I remember even talking to my therapist about it cuz I was like, "How can I speak out about this when I'm still doing it? Like, how can I bite the hand that feeds me and then like go online?" It was that like guilty conscious type of thing. So, I think I just felt so called to do it that I was like, "I feel called to do it for a reason." So, I just I did it once. And that's what's kind of brought a lot of opportunities. And then I started just getting more comfortable with speaking about it. And I was like, just trying to be honest of like, "Okay, yes, I'm speaking about this, but like also understand like I am still in the industry for these specific reasons. And I do have an exit plan, but I just I want to talk about this now." I mean, there's a lot to uncover. I could talk about it forever. So, yeah, I just I felt really called to do it and I just trusted that call. Yeah. Since you've started speaking out about this, are there specific stories or scenarios that you've shared that are you found people are so surprised to learn? I think pretty much everything. Like everyone just assumed that it was this great thing and that I was living this great life. And that's what it looks like on social media, you know? Like I was traveling Europe all summer. I was living in a beautiful apartment. I was moving to different cities. I moved to three different cities in 3 months. So, everyone was like, "Oh my gosh, she's really out here doing the absolute most." And so, I think people were shocked to hear about how what was going on behind the scenes. And yeah, I think pretty much everything possible. Like the illusion was really there for people that everything was fine. And it when now when I tell them, "Oh yeah, I I hated that and I dealt with this." They're like, "What? Like, how? How? You were doing all this stuff." And I'm like, "Yeah, but I was also dealing with this." Yeah. What does healing look like for you since leaving OnlyFans? I think healing, you know, it's convincing yourself or, you know, not even convincing yourself, but undoing all of the lies that you have already told yourself. So that like I won't make money doing something that I love. Or, you know, nobody's going to love me after what I've done. Or all men cheat. All men are like this. And having all of these ideas that come into your head after, you know, being in this industry, I think it's been realizing and also being around different crowds and, you know, not being around the people who are, you know, necessarily in the industry or still kind of, you know, glamored by it. So, I think it's that and just realizing like this isn't normal. And if it was normal, I would have felt good doing it. And trusting your body that was picking up on signals. And, you know, coming back into your body and also removing all these ideologies that you've picked up. I think another big one is also this like feeling of money coming in fast and understanding that, you know, things that are going to be worth your while that are going to take a long time. Doesn't mean they're going to take 10 years, but it might take, you know, a few months. And OnlyFans is so instant. So, it's also breaking these bad habits of believing that everything should just come to me immediately. And I should be able to do this immediately. And almost just like entitlement that you kind of pick up when you're in the industry cuz you're living in a fantasy land. So, I think healing has really been like uncovering all of the lies. And then I've definitely gotten stronger like in my faith, which I don't push, you know, Christianity or anything on anybody. I've always been very spiritual. So, I've kind of just incorporated that into my spiritual life and into my spiritual practice and trying to gain understanding of, you know, my journey, my purpose here, you know, why did I make these decisions? How can we not, you know, stay clear of making decisions that are unaligned for me in the future? And making it just this self-learning lesson, I think has also been very healing. And just learning how to understand yourself more. And then, yeah, I think body stuff is also important because when I went into the industry, I was definitely not confident in my body. But I think being in front of a camera so much, you kind of either have to fake it or you just have to learn to love your body. And so, that was probably one of the only, I guess, positives was learning to love my body through that. So, it's also just healing the relationship to your body. Um not and uncovering that like being an object thing cuz I was like in the same mindset a lot of girls are in of I'm just an object. Men just look at me to have sex with me, whatever. So, it's uncovering that as well and understanding that you're way more than a body and learning to love your body and cherish your body in whatever way that looks. And keeping it away from men who are are not going to treat you good in any capacity. So, yeah, that's that's definitely been a healing journey as well. Yeah, that's really beautifully said. Thank you for sharing that. For anyone who is considering joining OnlyFans, what would you say to them? What would you want them to know? Just believe in yourself enough to do what you're actually called to do. And that this is not a crutch. This is not a buffer. This is not going to give you the lifestyle that you want. There is an end to this and it's pretty fast. And you're going to end up having to start over anyways. So, learn to, you know, believe in yourself more, get comfortable with yourself, and play the long game. You know, things that start fast burn just as fast. And that was really, you know, what this is. There's no shortcuts to success. And you hear that in everybody's story. And the people who do try to shortcut it, they end up losing everything and having to start over anyways. So, just avoid that stuff. Avoid trying to, you know, go around the maze and just go right through it. And it's not going to be easy and it's going to suck. And you're going to see people that it seems like everyone's moving faster than you. And it is what it is. That's part of it. But you have to do it right. And if you do it right the first time, it'll be done right the first time. So, I think that's pretty much, you know, the the biggest thing, especially with the girls that are really young. And also, just the the whole lifestyle is all fake. You know, everything's fake. It's getting done by the whims and decisions being made by big corporations. And it's kind of crazy if you look at it now. It's become a normal expectation that we expect all these folks who are running businesses to become both the morality and legal police for the American society. Stokeley had had enough. 4 months later he stepped down in December 2021. There was a new CEO for a bit and then in 2023 they brought in a new CEO to run the company. That person well is a lawyer. That person's name is Key Blair and that tells you what's going to be the real battles for Only Fans going forward. Over the next four years, Radvinsky ran the company his way. It was totally out of the way. The company had one mission, and that was to put money in the pocket of Radvinsky. Over the next four years, the company has distributed $1.4 billion in cash to this one man alone. Even after all that distribution, the company was making $46 million per employee. Basically makes it more efficient per employee than almost any other company on the world. I mean, you go look at Google and Microsoft, Salesforce, Tesla, some of the great giant enterprises of the world, like they make nowhere near that. A lot of them are making 200 or 300,000 per employees, not 46 million. Reason is the business model. Only Fans has software that they run. They rent data centers and all they do is just take 20 cents of every dollar that flows through the platform. Creators do all the work creating the stuff. The customers break out their credit cards. While no politician would ever visit their office in the UK for good reason. In total, Only Fans pays more tax than Starbucks, IBM, and Microsoft combined each and every year, 167 million in the past year, for example. But remember the rise of all those kind of agencies that were creating fake relationships on behalf of creators and basically scamming the customers. Well, people started to notice this. Law firms have started to file class action lawsuits. The first few have been dismissed, but more and more are coming in. The public is starting to learn that the whole thing is kind of a scam. But that's not the biggest problem happening. The biggest disruption coming at this next point into 2022 and beyond is AI. On a positive note, the creators using AI scheduling tools and AI automation could stop needing these kind of scammy agencies to come in that were taking 30 to 50% of revenue. On the other half, well, the AI is coming for the creators themselves. You're starting to see the first fully AI generated artificial creators on the platform. People who are basically able now to run entire organizations and entire businesses on Only Fans without any real people involved at all. One platform called Fan View, a competitor to Only Fans, claims already that 15% of its revenue comes from AI generated creators. And one of those creators is named Itana Lopez. She makes €10,000 a month. The problem? Well, she's not actually really a Spanish woman. She's a fiction. She's totally AI generated. So that brings us to 2025. You have this underlying banking problem for Only Fans where there's any moment the possibility that the whole thing gets cut off from the financial institutions. You have this situation where customers are figuring out that the whole thing is basically a scam. You're talking to some call center in Pakistan, not a real person. And the technology is coming where AI promises to basically disrupt the entire thing. And remember, only fans exists because there is a marketplace being made of buyers and sellers. Why are all the buyers there, the viewers? Well, because that's where all the creators are. Where all the creators there? Well, that's because that's where all the buyers are. But when AI comes in and basically cuts all that out, the whole platform risks falling apart. Ravinsky is not stupid. In May 2025, almost a year ago, the news broke that he was exploring selling the business at an $8 billion valuation. If you took the exact numbers of revenue and growth that Only Fans was doing and made it a software company, that business might trade at 15 to 30 times earnings. Here was Radvinsky going out to try to sell it for 12 times earnings. So, Radvinsky was actually trying to sell it at a discount relative to that. But in practice, it would prove to be something else. And in the first kind of bidder to come through was an LA private equity firm. Uh its executives had tried to actually take Only Fans public in a spa a number of years before and that had failed. And here they were trying to put together a deal to buy the company at $8 billion. And a big part of that reason was the adult audience basically and content of the business makes the entire thing untouchable for huge pools of capital uh in the world. If you go look at private equity, hedge funds, a lot of those different types of funds, they are unable to put money into certain types of businesses. And one of those businesses is adult content. So while those folks might be willing to pay huge multiples for Salesforce or Microsoft stock, there's no way they're doing it for Only Fans. And you can see this across the industry for adult businesses. They throw off a ton of cash. Their buyers are few and far between. Pornhub, which is kind of the leading, I guess, YouTube for pornography, uh, basically sold in 2023 for $400 million. Not a huge multiple. Patreon, remember, basically the main street version of Only Fans? Well, it sold for $4 billion in 2021 despite never being profitable. You can see the dichotomy here. The deal with these LA investors, Forest Road at $8 billion. It fell apart. They couldn't put the financing together. Then just last month, the thing that precipitated this video creation was the Wall Street Journal reported that a new firm called Architect Capital had entered the picture and they were going to be paying $5.5 billion to buy Only Fans. Architect is a weird firm. Basically, they play in kind of oddball assets, tech enabled and stuff like Only Fans. The problem is they only have about $100 million under management according to public reports and they had to go find the rest of the money. And as I've watched posts on social media and people talk about this deal and remember Architect mostly does debt. They don't do private equity and they're just an oddball firm. There are people who are out there trying to raise money on their behalf to participate in the deal. And the stuff I read is that they're having a hard time doing it. Almost everybody with allocation or invites to invest in the deal doesn't want to have anything to do for all the reasons I talked about earlier in this video. Even still, Architect and his promoters are saying that they expect to fix the banking problem for Only Fans and the financial kind of Achilles heel that it has and then take the whole thing public in 2028. And look, nobody understands this business better than Ravinsky. He basically has pulled $1.4 billion out in dividends. But he sees all this stuff happening. He's been in the adult business for a very long time. It's crazy that after, you know, pulling out $700 million last year, he's pulling the plug and selling it what is not a very big multiple. And I think the only plausible answer for why he's doing that is cuz he sees all the headwinds and problems that are going to be coming for Only Fans in the future. And look, that's a pattern you see amongst really smart investors. They know to get out when the getting's good. And you saw that in my video that I did on Blockbuster. In fact, Blockbuster was sold twice by billionaires who knew exactly when to get out because they knew the future wasn't as bright as it had been when they got it. And to me, I think that's the big lesson of Only Fans. Well, there's probably two lessons. The first one is, look, there's a lot of money out there to be made if you're willing to compromise your values and ethics. For me, it's not worth it. I'd rather be broke than be in some of these businesses that people get in. But the second thing I think is, you know, you as a business owner, you as an investor, you have to pay attention to what's going around you and your business in the world. And in the case of Only Fans here, you see some folks doing exactly the right move, which is well, they're going to get out when they get good. And uh the future is not so bright for that business. Most locations, best selection, best prices, best loyalty programs, etc.

(but a fun fact there, they had to have a separate POS terminal to use for cannabis transactions, like only that station could do it, and that station couldn't do non-cannabis transactions...not sure why).

So, some investor (probably a hedge fund) in the US invested heavily in this successful Canadian business, and then put pressure on them to stop selling cannabis. Because in Canada, cannabis transactions can legally use the banking system, so it's not like how in some US states that are legalized, the transactions must be in cash and the stores have to physically transport that cash to and from the bank (a huge security risk, obviously).

So my guess is, the US investor or hedge fund, maybe ran into issues receiving their dividends from Fat Panda because the company sells cannabis (even though it's based in, located in, and operated in a foreign country with different laws).

And now Fat Panda's business has massively declined (a close friend works there, so I hear from her all the time about it), as many of their regular vape customers, who found it convenient that they could buy cannabis too at the same store, have taken their cannabis and vape business elsewhere, for convenience.
