But if this master plan for a data center opportunity zone is fully realized together with London County to the north, they'll have more data centers than Russia. Donna set up a local campaign to challenge the reasonzoning, but the lawsuit was dismissed. >> Yeah, it's heart-wrenching. It's heart-wrenching. Do I have ill feelings towards the data center industry for that? Yes, I do. I think we all need it. It's the way the world is going. But do I think dropping it next to people's homes is the right answer? No. There is no definitive public directory of data centers, no official map, no single regulator to ask or government agency to foyer. >> So, you know, it's it's been really tricky to kind of get these records because the companies don't want to disclose all of that information. >> By tricky, we mean redacted records and requests denied on the grounds of trade secrets. It turns out there is one thing that most data centers need. Backup generators in case the grid fails. And anyone who wants to install a generator needs to apply for an air quality permit. So what we set out to do was request all of the permits that are issued to data centers for those backup generators. >> That meant filing public record requests for air permits in every state. They list the capacity of the generator so we can extrapolate the power needs of the data center. They also provide clues about who owns it. Take this hotspot rapidly expanding close to Columbus, Ohio. There are at least 164 emergency generators permitted here. This is a data center where the air permit was applied for by an LLC called Mellin Enterprises LLC. Um, but the company has applied for a trade secrets uh exemption. So, they were actually able to redact pretty much all the information that we wanted. >> But all of the big tech giants must disclose any companies like LLC's that they own. And by digging into their official records, we managed to pull back the mask. And it turns out that the data center in Ohio, >> it's not Mellan Enterprises LLC. That's actually owned by Google. >> This is a task we repeated hundreds of times to build this map. It's the most comprehensive tally to date of America's exploding data center industry. Every dot is a data center large enough to need a permit for its backup generators. These are the facilities either already built or approved for construction at the end of 2024. We reached a total of,240 data centers. That's nearly four times the number in 2010. >> The companies that use the most amount of power in data centers across the country, it probably won't surprise you, are Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta, and QTS. Of the data centers we pinpointed, 177 belong to Amazon. And we've just passed the huge Amazon data centers behind us. And already we're straight into a residential area. And you can start to see why these data centers are so controversial. Those boxes on the roof there, that's the uh ventilation for the cooling systems to keep those hundreds and hundreds of servers from overheating and overloading. And these are running 24/7. And there's nowhere else in the world with a higher density of data centers than here in Lowden County, Virginia. As much as a third of the planet's internet traffic flows through the state of Virginia, the 329 data centers we tracked together consumed almost a quarter of the state's electricity in 2023. Tight security by the looks of it. We got pretty high fences with barb wire on top. I don't think we can get much closer. >> It's rare to get a glimpse inside one of these server warehouses. This large data center began emerging from the map south of Cheyenne in 2021. So we take the air from the above ceiling grid. We feed it down through the computers. They heat the air up. Comes back out the back and up through the chimney above uh ceiling and then just continually circulates the air. On that perspective, >> Lunari has the space to scale up to the more energy hungry GPU processes used to train AI algorithms. >> So this big white space is future computing. The cabinets in this facility now have a capacity of something like 5 to 10 kW. If they want to build the ones that are going to do AI with graphic processing units, GPUs, that'll go up to something like 70 to 100 kW. GPUs do need more power, but they can complete more tasks than a regular processing chip and in less time. The lower ambient temperatures in the high plains of Wyoming mean data centers use less energy and water to cool themselves. Lunari say they use around 500,000 gallons of water a month when at full capacity. That's roughly the amount of water used by 200 people. The nice cool ambient air temperatures allow us to maintain a low uh PUE uh which is a power usage efficiency which then allows us to just be a lower cost center for our customers and pass those savings on. But this area has been plagued by droughts. So farmers are watching the data center campuses emerging to the south of Cheyenne and getting nervous. This is a story unfolding not just in Wyoming and in Virginia, but across the country. The average American holds hundreds of gigabytes of data in the cloud. Big tech companies, the healthcare and finance sectors, governments, of course, hold much more. We're talking zetabytes of data being stored, processed, and retrieved every day. One zetabyte is 1 trillion GB. Of course, none of this lives up in the clouds. It's stored in large warehouses on the ground. This graph shows the explosion of data centers in the US in just the last 20 years. From above, just little white boxes, but remove the roof and you can see what all the fuss is about. Rows of computer servers performing calculations, training AI models, or storing data. Your social media accounts, photos, and videos live in a place like this. All these machines need power, a lot of it. So do the building's cooling systems and water pumps. The largest data centers can consume over 2 terowatt hours of electricity a year, enough to power 200,000 homes. And just in case the power cuts out, data centers have backup batteries as well as those diesel generators that they need permits for. The byproduct of all that energy use is heat. In many data centers, massive cooling systems suck out hot air and pass it through air conditioning units in a continuous loop. The most common type uses chilled water to absorb heat and release it from a cooling tower. The cooling systems and fans emit a constant drone. The noise level is generally below the limits permitted for industrial zones close to residential areas, but these were never designed with the 24/7 drone of modern data centers in mind. Listen to this. The ambient sound of Dulles Town Center in Northern Virginia recorded on a phone. Three Amazon data centers sit roughly 200 m away. I ran the audio through noise reduction software. Only then do you realize that hidden behind the drone of the data centers, there is bird song about 20 mi south in Manasses, Virginia. This has been his routine for the last few years. >> It's not something that you just hear. It's something that you just feel and enough to the point when it's really really strong, you can even hear the windows vibrate. >> He spent $20,000 on insulation and replacing all the upstairs windows. >> Still, the sound and the feeling of the constant hum keep him from sleeping. >> I'm getting bombarded each and every night. The American Public Health Association says chronic noise exposure can lead to serious health problems like cardiovascular disease or increased stress. As the Colorado River makes its way south from the Rockies, its precious water is siphoned off for agriculture, industry, and housing. Since 2000, river flow has shrunk by 20%. So, by the time it reaches Arizona, every drop counts. These maps show how extreme drought has gripped the state since 2000. Zoom out and you can see the entire Southwest is drying up. It's located in an area of extreme water stress and also we can see that the major basin that it's drawing most of its water is coming from the Colorado River. Documents show that Microsoft planned for each of these buildings to use 1 million gallons of water a day. A total of 1.83 billion gallons a year. That's enough water for roughly 61,000 Americans or a city the size of Santa Cruz, California. All this in a desert-like climate that is getting hotter and drier every year. >> And what we found is is that up to 43% of data centers, and this is our largest data centers, are located in areas of high or extremely high water stress. And that's really shocking because data centers require huge amount of drinking water to be able to cool their servers. >> You heard right, hundreds of thousands of gallons of drinking water. More than half of Microsoft's and nearly half of Amazon's data centers are in high water scarcity areas. In many cases, big tech companies have flocked to the desert to take advantage of a string of generous handouts, including tax breaks, affordable land, and cheap electricity. But trying to find out how much water these data centers need, has been difficult. >> A number of these permits um companies apply for what's called a trade secret exemption. But we've been excited to get around 50 records that really show the granular utility uh metered water use of some of the largest data centers in the country. >> Take this example, a Google data center campus in Midlotheian, Texas. Records show it used 160 million gallons of water in 2023, about the same as a small power plant. And this Kindrill data center outside Boulder, Colorado used 84.5 million gallons of water that same year. As this map shows, droughts are common here, too. In Arizona, water is tightly regulated, whether it comes from the Colorado River or is pumped from underground near population centers. But each smaller municipality can decide how it uses its water allocation. And in many parts of the state, there's little to stop companies from sinking their own wells. The big players, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta have pledged to be water positive by 2030, meaning they would restore or save more water than they use. But this is only possible via an elaborate system of water credits or offsetting. Basically, paying other people to save water or mitigate water pollution on their behalf. Another big player is QTS, a supplier of data centers where businesses can rent space for their IT infrastructure. According to our account, the company has 34 data centers in the US. QTS say they are investing heavily in water saving technologies. But not every aspect of their operations conserves water. This is a QTS data center in Aurora, Colorado. Some of these data centers are using more water to irrigate their grass outside than they are to cool their servers inside. An Aurora water official told us this one will need 1.1 million gallons of water a year just for this landscaping. Double the amount used by the building itself. And even if a data center manages to cut back water use, it's a trade-off. for example, you know, just air conditioning of some kind or closed loop systems, you're yeah, you're not going to use as much water, but you are going to substantially increase your overall power demand. So, it's kind of this balancing act where there's no real win here um in terms of what how resource intensive these data centers can be. If you add up all the power needs of the 1,200 plus data centers we tracked together, they could soon consume more than Poland did in 2023. Data centers, particularly driven by AI, could use as much as 600 terowatt hours of power by 2028. I mean, it's a magnitude that we've never really seen across the country before. It's really startling. As states race to fuel the AI boom, some are reversing their green energy promises and turning back to power from coal and natural gas. In Nebraska, the two largest electricity utility companies committed several years ago to net zero emissions from electricity generation by 2050. But just this single metadata campus in Springfield, Nebraska could use as much power in a year as 400,000 homes. One of the state's largest public utilities voted to postpone closing down two coal fired power plants here in Omaha. This image created using satellite data in June 2023 shows a plume of CO2 emissions released from the North Omaha station facility at an estimated rate of 300,000 kg per hour. And in 2025, the utility decided to build two new natural gas plants. All to meet spikes in electricity demand, mainly driven by data centers. And let's not forget the thousands of backup generators we tracked, which even if they run for just a few hours a month, spew harmful pollutants into the air. That means that utility providers are either abandoning their commitments or significantly stalling their commitments to move away from fossil fuel. Um they're not going towards renewable energy sources such as solar or wind, for example, because those resources right now can't sustain the massive power consumption demand. Big data center developers have announced massive investments in renewable projects like solar plants, wind farms, or nuclear power. For example, in Pennsylvania, Microsoft has struck a deal to buy power from the notorious 3mile island nuclear power plant when it reopens in 2027. The plant suffered a partial meltdown in 1979. But just like with the water credits, big tech companies are also looking to offset their carbon footprint by paying others to deliver renewable energy to the grid on their behalf. The big question is, can the already fragile and fragmented grid supported? And if massive infrastructure upgrades are needed, who picks up the tab? Amazon, Microsoft, and Google told Business Insider they were committed to paying their full share for upgrades to grid infrastructure like high voltage power lines. But there's plenty of evidence pointing to the fact that costs are already being passed on to customers. In Virginia, Dominion Energy disclosed that it would need to roughly double its electricity generation by 2039, mainly to meet demand from data centers and electric vehicles. The expansion could cost up to $103 billion, increasing residential electricity bills by as much as 50%. Despite the burden on the grid and on water supplies, some states are doing all they can to attract data centers into their backyards. Many regions simply don't want to miss out on the AI boom or the kudos of having a big tech employer on their doorstep. So far though, the promise of large numbers of jobs has not materialized. A Business Insider analysis found that even the largest data centers employ fewer than 150 permanent workers and some have as few as 25. But the tax breaks keep on coming. >> What we found is that massive corporations are benefiting from various taxes in different states for their data centers. We tracked 37 states offering tax incentive programs to data centers like zero tax on building materials, machinery or equipment, but also preferential rates on water and electricity. In Virginia, 56 data center projects received tax savings of almost a billion dollars in the 2023 fiscal year alone. Take a look at these data centers in New Orle. In 2017, a littleknown LLC called Sidecat went to the city of New Albany in Ohio and said, "We are going to build two gigantic data centers on about 300 acres of land. In return, could you please give us 100% property tax abayments for at least 15 years?" And the county said, "Okay." By our estimate, Scycat LLC received at least 60 million in fork on taxes. And then, you know, everybody found out that Scikut LLC is not a mom and pop a data center company, but was Meta, the parent company of Facebook. The roll out of AI across industries from social media to medical care will accelerate data center construction. In 2025 alone, Meta planned to spend at least $64 billion on facilities and equipment. Google's 75 billion and Microsoft 80 billion. And the ultra powerful computer chips driving the AI tools will consume more and more energy. A 2024 Department of Energy report estimates that their electricity use, driven by the AI boom, could soon require as much as 12% of total US electricity use. In 2023, it was just over 4%. For those like Donna living alongside these data farms, there are decisions to be made. >> I don't think a lot of my neighbors truly understand the severity of what's to come, and I'm hoping to be out before that happens. breaks my heart, but I can only fight for so long. And then I'm going to wave the white flag and I'm going to pack up and I'm going to leave Heat. Heat.

- What Is OnlyFans? Here’s What Every Parent Needs to Know: Kind: captions Language: en (calm music) - Have you heard of OnlyFans? It's been in the news recently, but many folks may not know exactly what it is or how dangerous it can be for children. Hi, I'm Caroline, a Senior Growth Marketing Manager at Bark, a parental controls company. Today, I'm going to explain how OnlyFans works, why it's so popular, and why it's definitely not for kids. OnlyFans is a website where people can post exclusive content that others can subscribe to. It has more than 130 million users and over a million content creators. Top earners on the platform can make hundreds of thousands of dollars each month. In terms of what the site looks like, think of it kind of like Facebook, but in order for someone to see your messages, photos, or videos, they'd have to pay you $5 a month or some other monthly price. Creators can upload videos they've filmed or they can broadcast live to their fans. They can also choose to provide their content in the subscription or pay-per-view model. But you're probably wondering, why would someone want to pay money for content when other sites like Facebook, YouTube, Instagram, and Spotify offer it for free? The answer is - it's complicated. There are plenty of content creators on OnlyFans that don't have anything to do with sex - like celebrities, musicians, and fitness instructors - but a lot of what draws subscribers to the site is adult content. In 2021, the site announced that it was going to ban explicit content, and quickly backtracked that decision, primarily because sexual content is what keeps the site afloat. Officially, you have to be 18 or older to sign up for an OnlyFans account because of all the sexual content. To subscribe to pages as a fan, even free accounts, you're asked to add a credit card. This is meant to help weed out kids, but it doesn't always work. Personal credit cards, prepaid debit cards, or even an unsuspecting parent's cards are often used to get past the restriction. To become a creator on OnlyFans, the verification process is more intense. This makes sense because creators will be making money from their content, which could include sexual activity. To share content, you're required to connect a bank account to receive payment. Then, to confirm your age, a third-party company takes a picture of your face. This will have to match to a form of ID you upload like a passport or driver's license. As you may have guessed though, this doesn't mean that it's impossible for minors to create an account. Or handed down, and kids can obtain bank accounts. If a child somehow manages to bypass all the restrictions and become an OnlyFans creator, they may face many dangers. They'll be able to post photos or videos that could be copied, downloaded, and shared in different places, all over the world, potentially forever. On the platform itself, kids could be contacted by other OnlyFans users and their subscribers, which could lead to predation, sextortion, and more, including aggressive and threatening messages from so-called fans. While OnlyFans consists primarily of amateur creators, the site is buzzworthy enough to have attracted the attention of quite a few celebrities. Cardi B has an account, as do pop culture figures like Aaron Carter, Amber Rose, DJ Khalid, and more. Some share adult content, but not all. Part of the appeal of celebrities having OnlyFans accounts is that fans get exclusive access to their lives that casual followers, say on Twitter or Instagram, don't get. Recently, the rapper Bhad Bhabie, you may remember from the "catch me outside" meme a few years back, made over $1 million in just six hours. We hope this video has helped you better understand what OnlyFans is as well as all of the dangers it presents to kids. If you're worried about your child stumbling across this website, Bark can help. Only Fans has changed the game. Launched in 2016, it's a platform where creators share exclusive content, fitness routines, cooking tips, art, music, or yes, sometimes adult content directly with their fans for a subscription fee. It's been a gold mine for many. In 2022 alone, the platform reportedly paid out over $5 billion to creators worldwide. From college students paying off loans to influencers diversifying their income, only fans promises control, independence, and big money. But here's the flip side. When you open your life to strangers online, you're not just inviting fans. Inviting anyone. And sometimes those anyone's have motives far darker than a monthly subscription. Asterisk story one. the stalker who knew too much or her first story centers on a creator will call Mia, a 24year-old graphic design student from a small town in Ohio back in 2020. Mia was drowning in student debt, $40,000 in counting. With part-time jobs barely covering rent, she turned to Only Fans after a friend raved about making thousands a month posting cosplay and lifestyle content. Mia wasn't into anything explicit. Her page was all about her love for anime, crafting detailed costumes, and sharing behind-the-scenes looks at her life as a creative. She set her subscription at $10 a month, posted regularly, and within three months had over 200 subscribers. The money was life-changing. He was a top tipper, dropping $50 or $100 on her posts with compliments like, "You're my favorite creator." Or, "This cosplay is perfection." At first, Mia was flattered. big tips meant she could buy better materials for her costumes, but loyal fan 92's messages started getting weirdly specific. One day, he wrote, "That blue shirt you wore last Tuesday looked amazing." Mia's stomach dropped. She hadn't posted anything about a blue shirt, and she never shared updates about her outfits. She brushed it off, thinking maybe she'd mentioned it in a story and forgotten, but the messages kept coming. Love how you always get coffee at Brood Awakening, he wrote another time. Brood Awakening was her local coffee shop, a place she visited almost daily, but she'd never mentioned it on Only Fans or anywhere online. Dot Mia started to panic. You also ideally want to do this with a creator who is still motivated to continue to make their own real content alongside the AI content that you're producing and not just 100% relying on AI content. And the reason why is because you want to include that authenticity factor and personality element that only the creator themselves can bring to their content. For example, personality based skit content, lifestyle content, and so on. Our goal at our agency is to run roughly a 70 to30 ratio of AI content to real content where 70% of the content that gets used on social media is AI content and the other 30% is real content. And keep in mind, guys, a lot of this AI content that gets used on Instagram, for example, ends up as trial reels, so it doesn't flood the account's feed. And while we're on the topic of Instagram, let me actually give you an example of what it looks like running this strategy on Instagram today. For example, let's say you're running Instagram and relying on 100% real content, which is fine, but just remember what a strain that puts on you, the creator you work with, and what an overall bottleneck this can become, especially when it comes to producing new viral trending content very, very quickly. Maybe you're posting one to two reels per day, and maybe every other day you're going to be posting a carousel post and then some lifestyle content mixed in all the time. Now add AI content production into the mix where you can multiply your content output without asking your creator to slave over more and more content every day to keep up. Let's say you make four reels per day using AI. If you pair that up with the two reels per day that your creator is already making, then you're already 3xing the production. And honestly, if perhaps all the AI reels you make aren't perfect, just post them as trial reels. You're going to still get views one way or the other. And if you don't want to [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] up the models page with any AI content that is somewhat questionable, but still want to use the content, trial reels are an amazing way to get that content used without posting suspect content to her main grid since trial reels won't be shown to the creator's followers. It's a win-win. Another strategy is Tik Tok. You can replicate current viral trends with AI and take advantage of that speed to production element that I keep referring to and then pay for Tik Tok promote a couple hundred worth and your odds of producing a viral Tik Tok go through the roof. Same thing with meta ads on Instagram or Facebook. You're basically cheating the system. Copy viral content, pay to boost it because you know it's already viral content and rinse and repeat. And this all these strategies and all these things that I'm talking about are great, but if making AI content just creates another manual system that you personally have to manage and learn yourself, it can become a big nightmare on its own. This is why you need to have a process of outsourcing this work. The entire process A to Z, ideiation, AI content creation, quality checks, scheduling, posting, all of it still needs to be outsourced eventually so that you aren't just sitting there at your computer all day making AI content for yourself. And quite frankly, outsourcing this specific type of work to someone else is probably one of the hardest things to outsource. the level of patience, knowledge, and creativity whoever it is that you're outsourcing this work to needs to have day in and day out to make AI content every single day. Even with these amazing tools at your fingertips, it's not easy, but it is possible. Trust me, talking as a person who is currently building out a system around this for our agency, day by day, it does get better and more realistic. And if you're a solo creator, book a call with us right now by DMing me on Instagram or Telegram, which both links are in the description of this video, and let's talk about working together, especially with this AI element. But that's pretty much it, guys. OFM truly is going from who can physically make the best content to who has the patience and knowledge and systems to make the best, highest quality AI content at a large scale. Business is always changing and we are currently witnessing that change right now within OFM. Appreciate you guys watching. Make sure you like, share, and subscribe. I'm out y'all.

It's going to make you talk to him differently and more personal because you know everything about him, which is then going to make him think that you actually care about him, which is therefore going to make him spend more money longterm. When it comes to the Only Fans wall posts, they should all be scheduled so you can get it out of your head. So just take a couple of hours, shoot like 50 to 100 pieces of content and then schedule these posts for the next couple months or so. Only fans wall post is something which is only going to distract you if it's always in the back of your head. So in a nutshell when it comes to your Only Fans profile, the only two things you're going to have to take care of is the messaging and the wall post scheduling. Now let's continue with Instagram. Apart from posting short form content, obviously you're going to have to be posting daily stories because your followers on IG are all potential subscribers that just have to see you every day for a bit longer until they finally subscribe. They need the feeling of knowing you to a certain extent. Maybe they just need to engage with you a couple of times before they subscribe. And stories are a key element of that process to happen. Go and add them to your close friend story so they feel like someone finally has acknowledged their existence. When it comes to Tik Tok, it's just about posting the short from content you've created and going live as many times you can. You can go live on Tik Tok for as many times and as long as you want. There's literally no limit. No stories or anything else needed on Tik Tok. Just post a short form content you've created. Go live for as many times as you can and be consistent with it. YouTube is posting only. No lives, no stories, nothing. Just post your short form content and gather all the data on your performance. Which leads me to the last point of this video. Data analysis and reasonable datadriven adjustments on your strategy. Datadriven changes are the only kind of changes you should ever consider to make because always chasing for the next big thing, always switching things up based on emotions is absolute garbage and it won't contribute to success. Which is the reason why we're doing data analysis. And basically what I want you guys to do is to track every single measurable value on your posts on Tik Tok, YouTube, and Instagram. And when I say all the data, I mean all of it. Likes, shares, saves, views, comments, watch time, retention rate, clicks onto the profile, everything. And after a time period of 6 weeks, you're going to have more than enough reliable data on each content vertical and how it was performing on all the different platforms. And this data is going to be more valuable than anything else. When it comes to this business, you'll understand content. You're going to understand the platforms you're working on, and you're going to be able to replace the bad performing content verticals, replace them with something new, and repeat the cycle. That way, it's going to be inevitable for the views to go up over time. And essentially, this is all you need to do to get an account to 10K a month. Get your marketing game right by doing enough research, sticking to your content verticals, and posting at least 30 times a week on each platform. Point number three, the account management. So, post daily stories on Instagram, go live as often as you can on Tik Tok, schedule the post on OF, and post short form content on all the short form platforms. And lastly, you get your performance data to redefine your content strategy. You probably don't want to understand how important and valuable this is, but I promise you it's going to help you so much more than anything else. Everyone can lay out a path in front of you. Everyone can teach you the basic principles, but you're going to be the one who actually has to initiate the process of learning the ins and outs of content, the ins and outs about marketing and the ins and out about this business as a whole. Overall, the biggest tip I can give you is to stop shielding your inaction with your alleged hunger for more knowledge and information. Just do the things I've told you to do without any exceptions and you'll probably see your account at 10K before even initiate in the second 6 week cycle. So, start now, put in the work.
